Conversely, if an abelian group $G$ has no elements of order $n$, then there exists a countable colouring of $G$ such that there is no monochromatic sumset $X+X$ for any subset $X \subseteq G$ of size $2$. What is the integer $n$?

Let $\Delta \ge 2$ be an integer. Consider an arbitrary graph of maximum degree $\Delta$ and a partition of its vertices into blocks of size at least $t$. An independent transversal is an independent set of the graph that contains exactly one vertex from each block. The reconfiguration graph of independent transversals has the independent transversals as vertices, and two such transversals are adjacent if they differ in exactly one block and their union is an independent set. What is the minimum value of $t$, as a function of $\Delta$, such that the reconfiguration graph of independent transversals is guaranteed to be connected for any such graph and partition?

Consider the Ising Curie-Weiss model on the complete graph, where the spin vector is constrained under a given $\ell^{p}$ norm for $p>0$. For $p>2$, there exists a critical inverse temperature $\beta_{c}(p)$ such that for $\beta<\beta_{c}(p)$, the magnetization is concentrated at zero and satisfies a Gaussian Central Limit Theorem, whereas for $\beta>\beta_{c}(p)$ the magnetization is concentrated at $\pm m_\ast$ for some $m_\ast>0$. What is the exact value of $\lim_{p\to\infty}\beta_{c}(p)$?

The inhomogeneous generalization of the Duffin-Schaeffer conjecture with a moving target considers a sequence of targets $\mathbf{y}_q \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and an error function $\psi:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$. It states that for almost every $\mathbf{x} \in\mathbb{R}^m$, there are infinitely many rational vectors $\mathbf{a}/q$ with each component of $\mathbf{a}$ coprime to $q$ such that $|q\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{a} - \mathbf{y}_q| < \psi(q)$. This is proven to hold in dimension $m \geq 3$ provided that a certain series $\sum_{q=1}^\infty S(q, m)$ diverges. What is the summand $S(q, m)$ as a function of $q$, $m$, $\psi(q)$, and the Euler totient function $\phi(q)$?

For a compact set $A \subset \mathbb{R}^n$, let the Hausdorff distance from $A$ to its convex hull $\text{conv}(A)$ be defined by $d(A) := \sup_{a \in \text{conv}(A)} \inf_{x \in A} |x - a|$, where $|x|$ is the standard Euclidean norm. The Dyn-Farkhi conjecture states that the function $d^2$ is subadditive on compact sets, meaning $d(A+B)^2 \leq d(A)^2 + d(B)^2$ for all compact sets $A, B \subset \mathbb{R}^n$. What is the largest integer dimension $n$ for which this conjecture holds true?

Consider a factor model data matrix $X = M + \mathbf{L}F + \sigma\Psi$, where the entries of the factor matrix $F$ and idiosyncratic noise matrix $\Psi$ are independent sets of i.i.d. random variables with mean zero and variance one, and the entries of $\Psi$ have finite fourth moments. Suppose the factor loading matrix $\mathbf{L}$ has fixed rank $r$, all its rows have a common Euclidean norm $L$, and the $r$ nonzero eigenvalues of $\mathbf{L}^\top\mathbf{L}$ tend to infinity as $N \to \infty$. Let the dimension $N$ of the data and the sample size $T$ tend to $\infty$ such that $N/T \to c > 0$. Let $\lambda_k$ denote the $k$-th largest eigenvalue of the sample correlation matrix $\mathbf{C}$. What is the almost sure limit of $\lambda_{r+1}$ as $N, T \to \infty$? Express your answer as a closed-form expression in terms of $c$, $L$, and $\sigma$.

In the Newtonian planar three-body problem, a syzygy is defined as a configuration where the positions of the three bodies are collinear, and a velocity syzygy is a configuration where their velocities are parallel. For a collision-free solution with negative total energy and zero angular momentum, what is the smallest integer $n$ such that every interval of time containing $n$ consecutive syzygies is guaranteed to also contain at least one velocity syzygy?

Let $n \ge 2$ be an integer. Consider the space of pairs of matrices $A,B\in GL_n(\mathbb{C})$ such that the eigenvalues of $A$, of $B$, and of the product $AB$ are specified in advance to generic values. What is the complex dimension of the space of such pairs $(A,B)$ under simultaneous conjugation by $GL_n(\mathbb{C})$, given as a function of $n$?

Let $M$ be the complement in $S^3$ of a $2$-bridge link $L$ with two components. The Thurston norm is a seminorm on $H_2(M, \partial M ; \mathbb{R}) \cong \mathbb{R}^2$, whose unit ball is a convex polygon. What is the maximum possible number of edges (i.e., $1$-dimensional faces) that this unit ball can have?

Consider the equation $\gamma_1^2 \pm \dots \pm \gamma_n^2 = 0$ for integral quaternions $\gamma_1, \dots, \gamma_n$. The number of such integral quaternion zeros at height at most $X$ admits an asymptotic formula of the form $c X^E + O(X^{3n+\varepsilon})$ for $n \ge 9$, some constant $c$, and any $\varepsilon > 0$. What is the exponent $E$ of the main term as a function of $n$?

Let $p \ge 1$ and $0 < k_1 < \dots < k_p < n$ be integers with $n \ge 17$. What is the minimal dimension of a Euclidean space into which the flag manifold $\operatorname{Flag}(k_1,\dots, k_p, \mathbb{R}^n)$ admits an $\operatorname{SO}_n(\mathbb{R})$-equivariant embedding?

Consider the set of all 30 MacMahon colored cubes, where each face is painted with one of six colors and every color appears on at least one face. A puzzle involves selecting a collection of eight distinct MacMahon cubes to build a $2\times 2\times 2$ model whose external coloring matches a specified target MacMahon cube, without the restriction that touching interior faces must be the same color. Over all possible collections of eight distinct MacMahon cubes, what is the maximum number of distinct target MacMahon cubes that can be built from a single collection of eight cubes?

Consider the Schrödinger equation $i\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \Delta u$ with radial initial data $u(x, 0) = f(x)$ on a Damek-Ricci space. Let $H^\alpha$ denote the inhomogeneous Sobolev space on this space. What is the sharp lower bound $\alpha_0$ on the regularity index $\alpha$ such that for initial data $f \in H^\alpha$, the solution $u(x,t)$ converges pointwise to $f(x)$ almost everywhere as $t \to 0^+$?

Let $k$ and $d$ be integers with $k > d \ge 2$, and let $n \ge 2k+2d-3$. For any family $\mathcal{F}$ of $k$-subsets of $\{1, \ldots, n\}$, define $m(\mathcal{F}) = \min_{D} |\{A \in \mathcal{F} \mid D \subseteq A\}|$, where the minimum is taken over all $d$-subsets $D \subseteq \{1, \ldots, n\}$. Over all intersecting families $\mathcal{F}$ of $k$-subsets of $\{1, \ldots, n\}$, what is the maximum possible value of $m(\mathcal{F})$?

Consider a sequence of graphs $G_n$ where the vertex set consists of all composite integers $c$ such that $4 \leq c \leq n$. Two distinct vertices in $G_n$ are connected by an undirected edge if and only if they are relatively prime. It is known that $G_n$ is a connected graph for all $n \geq 49$. What is the exact diameter (the maximum shortest path length between any pair of vertices) of the graph $G_n$ for any $n \geq 289$?

A subgroup $H$ of a finite group $G$ is defined to be weakly exponential if, for all $x \in G$, there exists $g \in G$ such that $x^{|G:H|} \in H^g$ (where $H^g$ denotes a conjugate of $H$ by $g$). If all subgroups of $G$ are weakly exponential, then $G$ is called wexp-solvable. Let $\pi(n)$ denote the number of primes less than $n$, and let $w(n)$ denote the number of primes $p$ less than $n$ such that the projective special linear group $\mathrm{PSL}(2,p)$ is wexp-solvable. What is the exact value of the limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{w(n)}{\pi(n)}$?

Consider the nonparametric problem of estimating an unknown model function $f$ from noisy observations of solutions to the ordinary differential equation $\dot{u} = f(u)$, where $u$ takes values in a $d$-dimensional state space and $f$ belongs to a Hölder-type smoothness class with smoothness parameter $\beta$. Let $n$ be the total number of observations. In the optimal asymptotic regime, the minimax optimal rate for the squared error of estimating $f$ scales as $n^\gamma$. What is the exponent $\gamma$ as a function of $\beta$ and $d$?

Consider the space $\mathbb{R}^n$ equipped with the $\ell_\infty$-norm. Two vectors $x, y$ in this space form a triangle equality attaining (TEA) pair if $\|x+y\| = \|x\| + \|y\|$. A linear map $T: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ is called a TEA preserver if for every TEA pair $x, y$, the vectors $T(x), T(y)$ also form a TEA pair. It has been proven that for exactly one integer dimension $n \ge 1$, there exist nonzero TEA preservers on $\mathbb{R}^n$ that are not a scalar multiple of an $\ell_\infty$-norm isometry. What is this exceptional dimension $n$?

Let $G=(V,E)$ be the $m \times n$ king graph, whose vertices represent the squares of an $m \times n$ grid and where two distinct vertices are adjacent if their coordinates differ by at most $1$ in each dimension. A subset $S \subseteq V$ is a dominating set if every vertex not in $S$ is adjacent to at least one vertex in $S$. Let $P_{m \times n}(z)$ denote the domination polynomial of $G$, defined as $\sum_{S} z^{|S|}$ where the sum is over all dominating sets $S$ of $G$. What is the exact value of $P_{m \times n}(-1)$ as a closed-form expression in terms of $m$ and $n$?

Let $S_{a,b}$ be the best constant for the Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg (CKN) inequality

u\in {\mathbb N}$,

as the residual norm $\left\|-\operatorname{div}(|x|^{-a}
abla u)-|x|^{-b(p+1)}|u|^{p-1}uight\|_{W^{-1,2}_a({\mathbb R}^d)}	o 0$. What is the value of the optimal stability power $\gamma$?

Let $\mathbb{D}$ be the unit disk in the complex plane, and let $B(z) = e^{is} \prod_{k=1}^d \frac{z-a_k}{1-z \overline{a_k}}$ be a finite Blaschke product of degree $d$. For $t \in [0,1]$, let $L(t)$ be the two-dimensional Lebesgue measure of the sublevel set $\{z \in \mathbb{D} : |B(z)| < t\}$. What is the sharp upper bound on $L(t)$ as a function of $t$ and $d$, which is attained for all $t \in (0,1)$ if and only if $a_k=0$ for every $k$?

Consider the supercritical Fujita equation $u_t = \Delta u + u^p$ where $p > \frac{n+2}{n-2}$ and $n \ge 3$. It is proven that the blow-up set of a non-negative type I blow-up solution to this equation, assuming the solution is not the spatially homogeneous solution $\kappa(T-t)^{-\frac{1}{p-1}}$ where $\kappa = (p-1)^{-\frac{1}{p-1}}$, is the union of an $(n-1)$-rectifiable set and a set of Hausdorff dimension at most $d$. What is the value of $d$ as a function of the spatial dimension $n$?

A simply connected proper subdomain $G$ of the unit disk $\mathbb{D}$ with locally connected boundary is called horocyclically convex if, for every $\omega\in \mathbb{D}\cap \partial G$, there exists a horodisk $H$ such that $\omega\in \partial H$ and $G\cap H=\emptyset$. An internal characterization states that $G$ is horocyclically convex if and only if any two distinct points can be joined inside $G$ by a $C^1$ curve composed with finitely many Jordan arcs whose constant hyperbolic curvature takes values in a specific open interval $(a,b)$. What is this interval $(a,b)$?

The spouse-loving variant of the uniform Oberwolfach problem asks whether $K_n+I$ (the complete graph of even order $n$ with a $1$-factor $I$ duplicated) admits a decomposition into $2$-factors, each of which is a disjoint union of cycles of length $m$. For such a decomposition to exist, it is necessary that $m$ divides $n$. For $m \geq 5$, it has been proven that this necessary condition is also sufficient, except for one specific family of pairs $(n,m)$ with $m$ being an odd integer, for which the problem remains open. For this unresolved family, what is $n$ expressed as a function of $m$?

The left and right diameters of a monoid are topological invariants defined in terms of suprema of lengths of derivation sequences with respect to finite generating sets for the universal left or right congruences. Let $C$ be an infinite chain (totally ordered set), and let $\operatorname{End}(C)$ be the endomorphism monoid of $C$ consisting of all order-preserving maps from $C$ to itself. What is the left diameter of $\operatorname{End}(C)$?

Consider an $n$-dimensional (with $n \geq 2$) constraint Hamiltonian system derived from the singular Lagrangian $L(N,q^k,\dot{q}^k) = \frac{1}{2N}g_{ij}\dot{q}^i\dot{q}^j - NV(q^k)$, where $N$ and $q^i$ are dependent variables on an independent parameter, and the dimension of the metric $g_{ij}$ is $n$. The geometric linearization of the equations of motion for this system is equivalent to the existence of exactly how many Noether symmetries? Express your answer as a function of $n$.

Let $\mathcal{G}$ be the class of all finite soluble groups $G$ with the property that every commutator in $G$ has prime power order. What is the maximum number of distinct prime factors that the order of the commutator subgroup $G'$ can have for a group $G \in \mathcal{G}$?
In the context of tropical geometry, what is the Krull dimension of the rational function semifield of a tropical curve that does not consist of only a single point?

Let $X$ be a smooth scheme over a perfect field $k$ of positive characteristic $p$, and let $i:Y	o X$ be a smooth closed subscheme of pure codimension $r$. Assuming resolution of singularities, there is a purity isomorphism for tame cohomology involving the logarithmic de Rham-Witt sheaves $
u_m(n)$ for integers $m, n \ge 0$. In the derived category of the tame site of $Y$, to what object is the extraordinary inverse image $Ri^! 
u_{X,m}(n)$ isomorphic? Express your answer in terms of $
u_m$ (representing the sheaf on $Y$), $n$, and $r$.

Two Fano planes defined on the same set of 7 points are said to be orthogonal if they share no lines. Let $F_1$ and $F_2$ be any two orthogonal Fano planes on the same set of 7 points. What is the order of their common automorphism group $\operatorname{Aut}(F_1) \cap \operatorname{Aut}(F_2)$?

Let $f(n,G|F)$ denote the smallest integer $m$ such that any edge coloring of the complete graph $K_n$ with at least $m$ colors forces a copy of $G$ in which each color class induces a member of $F$. Let $F_{\text{odd}}$ be the class of all graphs in which all vertices have odd degree. For $k \geq 5$, it is known that $f(n,K_k|F_{\text{odd}}) = (1+o(1))\text{ex}(n, K_r)$ as $n \to \infty$, where $\text{ex}(n, H)$ denotes the Turán number (the maximum number of edges in an $n$-vertex graph containing no subgraph isomorphic to $H$). What is the integer $r$ as a function of $k$?

For a simple graph $G$, an edge coloring of $G$ is called an improper interval edge coloring if for every $v\in V(G)$ the colors (which are integers) of the edges incident with $v$ form an integral interval. The interval coloring impropriety of $G$, denoted by $\mu_{int}(G)$, is the smallest integer $k$ such that $G$ has an improper interval edge coloring where at most $k$ edges of $G$ with a common endpoint have the same color. What is the maximum possible value of $\mu_{int}(G)$ over all simple outerplanar graphs $G$?

The odd cover number of the complete graph on $n$ vertices, $K_n$, is defined as the minimum size of a family of complete bipartite subgraphs of $K_n$ such that every edge of $K_n$ is contained in an odd number of these subgraphs. For an odd integer $n \ge 3$, what is the exact value of the odd cover number of $K_n$ as a function of $n$?

Let $R$ be a p.q.-Baer $*$-ring. It is a known theorem that the complement of the strong zero-divisor graph of $R$ is connected if and only if the $*$-ring $R$ contains at least $n$ central projections. What is the value of the integer $n$?

Let $M_{m,n}$ denote the space of $m 	imes n$ matrices over $\mathbb{R}$, equipped with the spectral norm $\|\cdot\|$. Two matrices $A, B \in M_{m,n}$ are called triangle equality attaining (TEA) if $\|A+B\| = \|A\| + \|B\|$. A non-zero linear map $T: M_{m,n} \to M_{m,n}$ is said to preserve TEA pairs if $T(A)$ and $T(B)$ are TEA whenever $A$ and $B$ are TEA. For integers $m, n \ge 2$, any non-zero linear map $T$ preserving TEA pairs is generally a positive multiple of a linear isometry (i.e., of the form $A \mapsto \gamma UAV$ or $A \mapsto \gamma UA^{t}V$ for a positive scalar $\gamma$ and orthogonal matrices $U, V$). However, there is exactly one ordered pair of dimensions $(m, n)$ with $m, n \ge 2$ for which there exist linear maps preserving TEA pairs that are not positive multiples of linear isometries. What is this exceptional ordered pair $(m, n)$?

Let $\Gamma$ be a hyperbolic group and $\partial\Gamma$ its boundary. Consider the boundary representation of $\Gamma$ on the compactly embedded function space $W^{\log,2}(\partial\Gamma) \subset L^2(\partial\Gamma)$, which is the domain of the logarithmic Laplacian on $\partial\Gamma$. Up to a multiplicative constant, what is the exact growth of the operator norm of the representation of an element $g \in \Gamma$ on $W^{\log,2}(\partial\Gamma)$, as a function of the word length $|g|$ of $g$?

An algebra over the complex numbers is called shift associative if it satisfies the identity $(xy)z=y(zx)$ for all elements $x, y, z$. What is the smallest possible dimension of a complex shift associative algebra that is not associative?

For a tensor $T \in \mathbb{C}^{n} \otimes \mathbb{C}^{n} \otimes \mathbb{C}^{n}$, its subrank is defined as the largest integer $r$ such that the diagonal tensor $I_r \in \mathbb{C}^{r} \otimes \mathbb{C}^{r} \otimes \mathbb{C}^{r}$ can be obtained from $T$ by applying linear maps $A, B, C: \mathbb{C}^n \to \mathbb{C}^r$ to the three tensor factors (i.e., $I_r = (A \otimes B \otimes C) T$). What is the exact value of the subrank of a generic tensor in $\mathbb{C}^{n} \otimes \mathbb{C}^{n} \otimes \mathbb{C}^{n}$ as a function of $n$?

Let $n$ be a positive integer such that $n \equiv 18 \pmod{24}$. What is the minimum number of complete bipartite subgraphs of the complete graph $K_n$ required such that every edge of $K_n$ is an edge of an odd number of these complete bipartite subgraphs?

Let $G$ be a connected graph. The edge-connectivity $\lambda(H)$ of a graph $H$ is the minimum number of edges whose removal renders $H$ disconnected. A graph $H$ is maximally edge-connected if $\lambda(H)$ equals its minimum degree. The square $G^2$ of $G$ is the graph with the same vertex set as $G$, in which two vertices are adjacent if their distance in $G$ is at most $2$. Let $\kappa(G)$ denote the vertex-connectivity of $G$, which is the minimum number of vertices whose removal renders $G$ disconnected. Assume $\kappa(G) \ge 2$. When $G^2$ is not maximally edge-connected, a lower bound on $\lambda(G^2)$ (which is sharp when $\kappa(G)$ is even) can be expressed as a polynomial $P(\kappa(G))$ in terms of the vertex-connectivity of $G$. What is the polynomial $P(x)$?

Let $n \geq 5$ be an integer, and let $S_n$ be the set of all integers $k$ such that there exists an $n$-element semilattice $(L,\vee)$ having exactly $k$ subuniverses (where a subuniverse is defined as a subset of $L$, including the empty set, that is closed under $\vee$). What is the fourth largest element of $S_n$, as a function of $n$?

Consider the AdaMDOF algorithm for distributed nonconvex finite-sum optimization. Let $n$ denote the sample size and $\epsilon$ denote the stationarity tolerance. It has been proven that AdaMDOF obtains a sample complexity of $O(n^a \epsilon^b)$ for finding an $\epsilon$-stationary solution. What is the ordered pair $(a, b)$?

Let $X$ be a smooth, complex Fano 4-fold admitting a rational contraction onto a 3-fold (i.e., a rational map $X \dashrightarrow Y$ that factors as a sequence of flips $X \dashrightarrow X'$ followed by a surjective morphism $X' \to Y$ with connected fibers, where $Y$ is a normal projective variety of dimension 3). Suppose $X$ is not isomorphic to a product of del Pezzo surfaces. What is the maximum possible value of the Picard number $\rho(X)$?

Let $\xi(x)$ for $x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$ be the integral kernel of stochastic currents corresponding to a $d$-dimensional fractional Brownian motion with Hurst parameter $H\in(0,1)$, defined within the framework of white noise analysis. For dimension $d\ge 2$, the kernel evaluated at the origin, $\xi(0)$, is a Hida distribution if and only if $H$ is restricted to the interval $(0, H_{\max})$. What is the exact value of $H_{\max}$ as a function of $d$?

Let $\epsilon_1, \ldots, \epsilon_n$ be independent Rademacher random variables (taking values in $\{-1, 1\}$ with equal probability). What is the infimum of the set of real numbers $R > 0$ such that there exists a universal constant $c > 0$ for which

$$ \Pr\left[\left\|\sum_{i=1}^n \epsilon_i v_i\right\|_2 \leq R\right] \geq \frac{c}{n} $$

for all positive integers $n$ and all finite sequences of unit vectors $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$?

Let $p \ge 11$ be a prime number with $p \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$ and let $n \ge 1$ be an integer. It is known that a sum-free subset of the vector space $\mathbb{F}_p^n$ can have size at most $\frac{1}{3}(p+1)p^{n-1}$ and that, up to automorphisms of $\mathbb{F}_p^n$, the only extremal example is the set $E = \left[\frac{p+1}{3}, \frac{2p-1}{3}\right] \times \mathbb{F}_p^{n-1}$. What is the maximum possible size of a sum-free subset of $\mathbb{F}_p^n$ that is not contained in $\phi(E)$ for any automorphism $\phi \in \mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{F}_p^n)$?

In the analysis of the Annealed Sinkhorn algorithm for solving optimal transport problems, the inverse temperature parameter is updated according to a non-decreasing schedule $(\beta_t)_{t\in \mathbb{N}}$, where $t$ is the iteration count. The error of the algorithm at iteration $t$ can be decomposed into an 'entropic' error (which scales as $\Theta(\beta_t^{-1})$) and a 'relaxation' error induced by the annealing procedure. What is the exponent $p$ such that the schedule $\beta_t = \Theta(t^p)$ achieves the optimal asymptotic error trade-off between these two terms?

Let $\mathcal{A}_{2n}$ be the set of up-down permutations of length $2n$, i.e., permutations $\pi_1 \pi_2 \cdots \pi_{2n}$ of $\{1, 2, \dots, 2n\}$ such that $\pi_1 < \pi_2 > \pi_3 < \pi_4 > \dots < \pi_{2n}$. Let $\text{rlmax}(\pi)$ denote the number of right-to-left maxima of $\pi$. What is the closed-form expression for the exponential generating function $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( \sum_{\pi \in \mathcal{A}_{2n}} q^{\text{rlmax}(\pi)} \right) \frac{t^{2n}}{(2n)!}$?

In quantum information theory, the quantum Chernoff exponent characterizes the asymptotic error probability in hypothesis testing. Consider the generalized task of discriminating among an arbitrary, a priori known library of reflective targets. In the limit of low transmitter brightness, high loss, and high thermal background, let $E_{\mathrm{QI}}$ be the Chernoff exponent of the error probability when using a Gaussian-state entangled quantum illumination (QI) probe, and let $E_{\mathrm{CI}}$ be the Chernoff exponent when using classical coherent-state illumination (CI) of the same brightness. What is the asymptotic value of the ratio $\frac{E_{\mathrm{QI}}}{E_{\mathrm{CI}}}$?

Let $ex(n, \mathscr{F})$ denote the maximum number of edges in an $n$-vertex graph that does not contain any graph from the family $\mathscr{F}$ as a subgraph. Let $K_{2,t}$ denote the complete bipartite graph with part sizes 2 and $t$, and let $M_{s+1}$ denote a matching consisting of $s+1$ independent edges. For an integer $t \ge 3$ and integers $s$ and $n$ both sufficiently large, what is the exact value of $ex(n, \{K_{2,t}, M_{s+1}\})$ as a function of $n, t,$ and $s$?

Consider a three-dimensional fluid-particle coupled model in the whole space $\mathbb{R}^3$ combining the full compressible Navier-Stokes equations with the Vlasov-Fokker-Planck equation via momentum and energy exchanges. For initial data that are a small perturbation of the equilibrium in the Sobolev space $H^2$ and additionally small in the space $\mathcal{Z}_1$ (where the perturbation is in $L^2_v(L^1_x)$ for the particle distribution function and $L^1_x$ for the fluid variables), the $L^2$-norms of the strong solutions and their spatial gradients are proven to decay at optimal time rates of $(1+t)^{-3/4}$ and $(1+t)^{-5/4}$, respectively. The optimal time decay rate of the $L^2$-norm of the highest-order spatial derivatives of these strong solutions is given by $(1+t)^{\alpha}$. What is the value of $\alpha$?

Consider the consumption chemotaxis problem
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t = \Delta u - \chi\nabla \cdot (u\nabla v) + \lambda u - \mu u^2 - c \lvert \nabla u \rvert^\gamma, \\
v_t = \Delta v - uv,
\end{cases}
\]
in a bounded and smooth domain $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$, $n\geq 3$, under Neumann boundary conditions, with parameters $\chi,\lambda,\mu,c>0$ and positive initial data $u_0,v_0$ with sufficient regularity. It has been established that the problem has a unique and uniformly bounded classical solution independent of any additional assumptions on the parameters or initial data when the exponent $\gamma$ belongs to the interval $(\gamma_{\text{crit}}(n), 2]$. For $\gamma = \gamma_{\text{crit}}(n)$, the same boundedness result holds only under an additional condition involving the parameters $c,\mu,n,\chi$ and the initial data. What is the explicit formula for the critical exponent $\gamma_{\text{crit}}(n)$?

Let a topological 2-page book embedding of a graph be an embedding where all vertices are placed on a straight line (the spine) and every edge is drawn in the two half-planes separated by the spine such that it crosses the spine at most once. A Kleetope is a plane triangulation derived from an arbitrary plane triangulation $T$ by inserting a new vertex into each face of $T$ and connecting it to the three vertices of that face. It is known that every $n$-vertex Kleetope admits a topological 2-page book embedding with at most $k$ edges crossing the spine, and this upper bound $k$ is tight over the family of all $n$-vertex Kleetopes. What is the tight bound $k$ as a function of $n$?

Consider the families of $\mathrm{su}(m|n)$ supersymmetric spin chains of Haldane-Shastry type related to the $A_{N-1}$ and $BC_N$ classical root systems, excluding the Frahm-Inozemtsev chain with $\gamma=0$. The low-temperature behavior of their thermodynamic free energy per spin corresponds to that of a $(1+1)$-dimensional conformal field theory. What is the central charge $c$ of this conformal field theory, expressed as a function of $m$ and $n$?

Let $T^*(r, n)$ be the $n$-vertex graph obtained by taking the complete bipartite graph $K_{\lfloor\frac{n-r+1}{2}\rfloor, \lceil\frac{n-r+1}{2}\rceil}$ and a complete graph $K_r$, and identifying exactly one vertex of the bipartite graph with one vertex of the $K_r$. Let $k$ and $r$ be integers with $3\le r\le 2k-4$, and let $n\ge 20(r+2)^2k$. Let $\mathcal{G}$ be the family of all $n$-vertex graphs $G$ that do not contain a cycle of length $2k+1$ and have at least as many edges as $T^*(r, n)$. Let $\gamma_2(G)$ denote the minimum number of edges that must be removed from $G$ to make it bipartite. What is the maximum value of $\gamma_2(G)$ over all $G \in \mathcal{G}$, expressed as a function of $r$?

Every polygon with $n$ vertices in the complex projective plane is naturally associated with its adjoint curve of degree $n-3$. In particular, the adjoint of a heptagon ($n=7$) is a plane quartic. What is the exact number of distinct complex heptagons, up to the action of the dihedral group on the labels of the lines, that have a general plane quartic as their adjoint?

Let $X$ be a projective hyperkähler manifold of K3$^{[n]}$-type with $n \geq 2$. What is the minimum integer $k$ such that every such manifold $X$ with Picard number $\rho(X) \geq k$ is guaranteed to be isomorphic to a moduli space of twisted stable sheaves on a K3 surface?

Consider heterochaos baker maps whose central direction is mostly neutral. It has been proven that the correlations for Hölder continuous functions decay at an optimal polynomial rate of order $n^p$ as $n \to \infty$. What is the exact value of the exponent $p$?
Let $\kappa$ be an infinite cardinal and let $G$ be a $\kappa$-existentially closed group of cardinality $\kappa$. What is the cardinality of the automorphism group $\operatorname{Aut}(G)$?

Consider the stationary sonic-subsonic solutions $\rho$ to the one-dimensional unipolar isothermal hydrodynamic model of semiconductors (Euler-Poisson equations) on the spatial interval $(0,1)$ with a sonic boundary. It has been proven that there exists a critical Sobolev exponent $p_0$ such that the solution $\rho$ belongs to the Sobolev space $W^{1,p}(0,1)$ for any $p < p_0$, but $\rho \notin W^{1,p}(0,1)$ for any $p \ge p_0$. What is the exact value of $p_0$?

The balance game is played on a graph $G$ of order $n$ by two players, Admirable (A) and Impish (I), who take turns selecting unlabeled vertices of $G$. Admirable labels the selected vertices by $0$ and Impish by $1$, and the resulting label on any edge is the sum modulo $2$ of the labels of the vertices incident to that edge. Let $e_0$ and $e_1$ denote the number of edges labeled by $0$ and $1$ after all the vertices are labeled. The discrepancy in the balance game is defined as $d = e_1 - e_0$. The two players have opposite goals: Admirable attempts to minimize the discrepancy $d$ while Impish attempts to maximize $d$. When (A) makes the first move in the game, the (A)-start game balance number, $b^A_g(G)$, is the value of $d$ when both players play optimally. When (I) makes the first move in the game, the (I)-start game balance number, $b^I_g(G)$, is the value of $d$ when both players play optimally. What is the exact value of $b^A_g(G) + b^I_g(\overline{G})$ as a function of $n$, where $\overline{G}$ is the complement of $G$?

Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N\times n}$ be a rectangular random matrix whose entries are independent and identically distributed symmetric random variables with tail probability $\mathbb{P}(|x_{ij}|>t)\sim t^{-\alpha}$ for some parameter $\alpha>0$. Consider the asymptotic regime where $N(n)/n\to \mathsf{a}>1$ as $n \to \infty$. Let $\mathfrak{u}$ be the right singular vector corresponding to the smallest singular value of $X$. There is a phase transition in the localization length of $\mathfrak{u}$: there exists a critical value $\alpha_c$ such that for $\alpha < \alpha_c$, the localization length of $\mathfrak{u}$ is $O(n/\log n)$, and for $\alpha > \alpha_c$, the localization length is of order $n$. What is the exact value of $\alpha_c$?

The power graph of a finite group $G$ is the simple undirected graph with vertex set $G$, in which two distinct elements are adjacent if and only if one is an integer power of the other. The oriented diameter of a $2$-edge-connected undirected graph is the minimum diameter among all its strongly connected orientations. For any integer $n > 6$, what is the exact oriented diameter of the power graph of the cyclic group of order $n$?

Let $d \geq 2$ be an integer, $s \in (0,1]$, and $t \in [0,2(d-1)]$. Suppose a set $X \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ has the property that there exists a collection of lines in $\mathbb{R}^d$ of packing dimension $t$ such that every line from the collection intersects $X$ in a set of packing dimension at least $s$. What is the sharp lower bound on the packing dimension of $X$ in terms of $s$ and $t$?

Consider an $n$-vertex graph $G$ and a vertex $v$ in $G$. Let $w_k(v)$ denote the number of walks of length $k$ starting from $v$. It has been shown that the entire sequence $(w_k(v))_{k \ge 0}$ is completely determined by the counts for the first $N$ shortest lengths. Furthermore, this integer bound $N$ is asymptotically tight. What is the value of $N$ as a function of $n$?

Let $G_{\mathbb R}=PSO(2n-1,1)$. The stalks of the intersection cohomology ($\text{IC}$)-complexes for spherical orbit closures in the real affine Grassmannian for $G_{\mathbb R}$ are given by the Kostka-Foulkes polynomials for $GL_2$, but with the standard parameter $q$ replaced by a monomial in $q$ depending on $n$. What is this monomial?

Let $\mathcal{D}$ be a nontrivial symmetric $(v,k,\lambda)$ design where $\lambda$ is a prime number. Suppose that $\mathcal{D}$ admits an automorphism group $G$ that is flag-transitive and point-primitive of affine type. If $G$ is not a subgroup of $\mathrm{A\Gamma L}_1(q)$ for any odd prime power $q$, what must be the exact parameter set $(v,k,\lambda)$ of the design $\mathcal{D}$?

A $k$-kernel in a digraph $G$ is a stable set $X$ of vertices such that every vertex of $G$ can be reached from $X$ by a directed path of length at most $k$. A split digraph is a digraph whose vertex set can be partitioned into a tournament and a stable set. Erdős and Székely conjectured in 1976 that every digraph $G$ with no source has a 2-kernel $K$ whose size is bounded by $c |V(G)|$. This conjecture was later proven for the class of split digraphs (improving an earlier bound of $\frac{2}{3}|V(G)|$). What is the exact value of the constant $c$ in the Erdős-Székely conjecture?

Let $d,k$ be natural numbers and let $\mathcal{L}_1, \dots, \mathcal{L}_k \in \mathrm{GL}_d(\mathbb{Q})$ be linear transformations such that there are no non-trivial subspaces $U, V \subseteq \mathbb{Q}^d$ of the same dimension satisfying $\mathcal{L}_i(U) \subseteq V$ for every $1 \leq i \leq k$. It is proven that for every non-empty, finite set $A \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, the cardinality $|\mathcal{L}_1(A) + \dots + \mathcal{L}_k(A) |$ is bounded below by an expression of the form $C |A| - O_{d,k}(|A|^{1- \delta})$, where $\delta>0$ is a constant depending on $d$ and $k$. What is the exact value of the coefficient $C$ as a function of $d$ and $k$?

The extended mapping class group of a surface is defined to be the group of isotopy classes of (not necessarily orientation-preserving) homeomorphisms of the surface. There is exactly one positive integer $n$ such that the extended mapping class group of an $n$-punctured sphere is NOT generated by two elements of finite order. What is this value of $n$?

For an abelian group $G$ and subsets $A \subseteq D \subseteq G$, define $\ell_D(A) := \min \{ |B| : B \subseteq D, \, A \subseteq B+B \}$. For subsets $A \subset \mathbb{Z}$, suppose $\ell_{\mathbb{Q}}(A) = n$. It is known that there is a constant $c$ such that $\ell_{\mathbb{Z}}(A) \le c n$ for all such subsets $A$, and that this constant is optimal in the sense that for any integer $n$, there exist sets $A \subset \mathbb{Z}$ with $\ell_{\mathbb{Q}}(A) = n$ and $\ell_{\mathbb{Z}}(A) \ge c n - O(\sqrt{n})$. What is the value of the constant $c$?

Let $D$ be an oriented multigraph (a directed multigraph without loops and directed $2$-cycles). Let $\operatorname{fas}(D)$ denote the minimum size of a feedback arc set in $D$. The degree of a vertex in $D$ is defined as the sum of its out-degree and in-degree. What is the supremum of the ratio $\frac{\operatorname{fas}(D)}{m}$ over all oriented multigraphs $D$ with $m \ge 1$ arcs and maximum degree at most $5$?

In topological dynamics, an $m$-to-one topomorphic extension is a system whose factor map to its maximal equicontinuous factor is measure-theoretically at most $m$-to-one, for an integer $m \ge 1$. It has been proven that every minimal $m$-to-one topomorphic extension exhibits a specific multivariate mean equicontinuity property, namely that it is mean $k$-equicontinuous. What is the exact value of the integer $k$ expressed as a function of $m$?

Let $L_n$ denote the $n$-th term in the Lucas sequence, defined by the initial values $L_0 = 2$, $L_1 = 1$, and the recurrence relation $L_n = L_{n-1} + L_{n-2}$ for $n \ge 2$. What is the unique pair of non-negative integers $(n, x)$ that satisfies the Diophantine equation $L_n^2 + L_{n+1}^2 = x^2$?

Consider a Neumann optimal boundary control problem posed on a convex polyhedral domain, fully discretized using piecewise linear and globally continuous functions for the control and standard linear finite elements for the state. There exists a critical threshold for the largest interior edge angle such that, if the largest interior edge angle is strictly less than this threshold, the discrete controls achieve a convergence rate of 2 (up to a logarithmic factor) in the $L^2$-norm on the boundary, whereas for larger angles the convergence rate is reduced due to singular domain-dependent terms. What is this critical angle?

Let $\text{CR}(s,r)$ be the unordered asymmetric Canonical Ramsey number, defined as the minimum integer $n$ such that every edge-coloring of the unordered complete graph $K_n$ contains either a rainbow clique of order $r$, or an orderable clique of order $s$. It has been established that for fixed $s$, the asymptotic growth rate of $\text{CR}(s,r)$ as $r \to \infty$ is $\Theta\left(\left(\frac{r^3}{\log r}\right)^k\right)$. What is the exact value of the exponent $k$ as a function of $s$?

Let $R(G,H)$ denote the Ramsey number, defined as the minimum integer $N$ such that every red-blue edge coloring of the complete graph $K_N$ contains either a red subgraph isomorphic to $G$ or a blue subgraph isomorphic to $H$. Let $C_4$ denote the cycle graph on $4$ vertices, and let $K_{1, 27}$ denote the star graph with a central vertex and $27$ leaves. What is the exact value of the Ramsey number $R(C_4, K_{1, 27})$?

Given two graphs $H$ and $G$, the induced Turán number ${\rm ex}(n; H, G{\rm -ind})$ is defined as the maximum number of edges in an $n$-vertex graph having no subgraph isomorphic to $H$ and no copy of $G$ as an induced subgraph. Let $C_k$ denote the cycle graph on $k$ vertices and $K_{1,t}$ denote the star graph with $t$ leaves. For integers $t \geq 3$ and $k \geq 3$, there is a function $f(n, k, t)$ of the form $c \cdot n$ (where $c$ depends only on $k$ and $t$) such that ${\rm ex}(n; C_k, K_{1,t}{\rm -ind}) \leq f(n, k, t)$ for all $n$, and this bound is achieved with equality for infinitely many values of $n$. What is $f(n, k, t)$?

Consider mild solutions to the one-dimensional stochastic heat equation driven by space-time white noise, with a superlinear accretive forcing and a polynomially growing multiplicative noise term. What is the critical exponent $\gamma$ such that if the multiplicative noise grows faster than $|u|^\gamma$, the solution is known to explode?

Consider the simple exclusion process with $k$ particles on the line segment $[1, N]$, where the rate of swapping the contents of sites $x$ and $x+1$ is given by the conductance $c^{(N)}(x, x+1)$ for $1\le x<N$. Let $r^{(N)}(x, x+1) := 1/c^{(N)}(x, x+1)$ be the corresponding resistance. Assume that
\[ \limsup_{N\to \infty}\, \frac{1}{N}\sup_{1< m \le N}\, \left| \sum_{x=2}^m r^{(N)}(x-1, x)- (m-1) \right| = 0\,, \]
and that further necessary regularity conditions on $r^{(N)}$ and $k$ are satisfied. It is proven that the total variation distance to equilibrium drops abruptly from $1$ to $0$ at a cutoff time $t_c(N, k)$. What is the exact leading-order term for $t_c(N, k)$ (i.e., the term $T(N,k)$ such that $t_c(N,k) = (1+o(1))T(N,k)$)? Express your answer in terms of $N$, $k$, and fundamental constants.

In the context of multi-channel sub-Nyquist spectral estimation driven by the Unlimited Sensing Framework (USF) that aliases samples in both amplitude and frequency domains, an exact recovery theorem establishes that a signal composed of $K$ sinusoids of arbitrary amplitudes and frequencies can be perfectly recovered from a specific number of modulo samples, independent of the sampling rate or folding threshold. What is this sufficient number of modulo samples given as a function of $K$?

Let $\pi$ be a cyclic permutation of length $n$ in the symmetric group $S_n$. The one-line form of $\pi$ is the sequence $\pi_1 \pi_2 \dots \pi_n$. Since $\pi$ is an $n$-cycle, it can be written in cycle notation as $(c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n)$ in exactly $n$ ways. Each such representation gives a sequence $c_1 c_2 \dots c_n$, called a cycle form of $\pi$. Let $\mathcal{A}^\circ_n(\sigma; \tau)$ denote the set of cyclic permutations in $S_n$ that avoid the pattern $\sigma$ in their one-line form and avoid the pattern $\tau$ in all $n$ of their cycle forms. For $n \ge 2$, what is the cardinality $|\mathcal{A}^\circ_n(2431; 1324)|$? Express your answer in terms of $P_k$, the $k$-th Pell number (with $P_0=0$, $P_1=1$, and $P_k = 2P_{k-1} + P_{k-2}$ for $k \ge 2$).

Let $\mathbb{F}_q$ be a finite field of order $q$, and let $n$ and $l$ be positive integers with $n \ge l$. What is the maximum possible size of a family of monic polynomials in $\mathbb{F}_q[x]$, each of degree $n$, such that every pair of polynomials in the family shares a common factor in $\mathbb{F}_q[x]$ of degree at least $l$?

It has been proven that for any $\varepsilon > 0$, the $k$-Dimensional Matching problem is hard to approximate within a factor of $\frac{k}{C + \varepsilon}$ for large $k$ unless $\mathsf{NP} \subseteq \mathsf{BPP}$. This lower bound improves upon the previous $\Omega(k/\log k)$ hardness bound and is achieved using an approximation-preserving gadget from $R$-degree bounded $k$-CSPs over alphabet size $R$ to $kR$-Dimensional Matching. What is the value of the integer $C$?

Let $n$ be an odd positive integer. Among all Eulerian orientations of the complete graph $K_n$ (i.e., regular tournaments on $n$ vertices), what is the minimum possible total number of spanning arborescences (where the total is summed over all possible choices of the root vertex)? Provide the answer as a closed-form expression in terms of $n$.

Let $N\geq 5$, $\alpha>2-N$, and $\frac{N-4}{N-2}\alpha-4 \leq \beta\leq\alpha-2$. Consider the high order Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg type inequality
\[ \int_{\mathbb{R}^N}|x|^{-\beta}|\mathrm{div} (|x|^\alpha\nabla u)|^2 \mathrm{d}x \geq \mathcal{S}\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}^N}|x|^\gamma |u|^{2^{**}_{\alpha,\beta}} \mathrm{d}x\right)^{\frac{2}{2^{**}_{\alpha,\beta}}} \]
for all $u\in C^\infty_0(\mathbb{R}^N\setminus\{0\})$, where $\mathcal{S}$ is the best constant and $2^{**}_{\alpha,\beta}:=\frac{2(N+\gamma)}{N+2\alpha-\beta-4}$ with $(N+\beta)(N+\gamma)=(N+2\alpha-\beta-4)^2$.
Through the analysis of the linearized problem related to the radial extremal, there exists a symmetry breaking threshold $\beta_{\mathrm{FS}}(\alpha)$ such that for $\alpha>0$ and $\frac{N-4}{N-2}\alpha-4<\beta<\beta_{\mathrm{FS}}(\alpha)$, the extremal functions for $\mathcal{S}$ are nonradial.
What is the explicit expression for $\beta_{\mathrm{FS}}(\alpha)$ as a function of $N$ and $\alpha$?

Let $G$ be a graph of order $n$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \ge \cdots \ge \lambda_n$. Define $s^+(G) = \sum_{\lambda_i>0} \lambda_i^2$ and $s^-(G) = \sum_{\lambda_i<0} \lambda_i^2$. The square energy of $G$ is $s(G) = \min\{s^+(G), s^-(G)\}$. It is a theorem that if $H_1, \ldots, H_k$ are disjoint vertex-induced subgraphs of $G$, then $s^+(G) \ge \sum_{i=1}^k s^+(H_i)$ and $s^-(G) \ge \sum_{i=1}^k s^-(H_i)$. By applying this theorem, a linear lower bound of the form $s(G) \ge c n$ is established for every connected graph $G$ of order $n \ge 4$. What is the value of the constant $c$?

Let $V$ be a hyperbolic plane over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$ of order $q$. For which values of $q$ is it true that all elements of the special orthogonal group $\mathrm{SO}(V)$ are bireflectional (that is, can be expressed as a product of two involutions)?

Let $U_t(a,q)$ be defined as the sum over integers $n_1, \dots, n_t$ such that $1 \leq n_1 < n_2 < \dots < n_t$:
$$U_t(a,q) := \sum_{1 \leq n_1 < n_2 < \cdots < n_t} \prod_{i=1}^t \frac{q^{n_i}}{1+a q^{n_i} + q^{2n_i}}$$
It has been shown that for $t=2$ and $a=1$, the function $U_2(1,q)$ can be expressed exactly as $U_2(1,q) = \sum_{n=1}^\infty R(q^{3n})$, where $R(x)$ is a rational function. Determine the rational function $R(x)$.

Let $\{F_n\}$ be a sequence of random variables belonging to the second Wiener chaos of an isonormal Gaussian process. It has been established that $\{F_n\}$ converges in distribution to a bilateral-gamma random variable $X \sim BG(\alpha_1, p_1, \alpha_2, p_2)$ satisfying $\alpha_1 \alpha_2 > 1 + |\alpha_1 - \alpha_2|$ and $\frac{p_1}{p_2} = \frac{\alpha_1}{\alpha_2}$, provided that the cumulants of $F_n$ of order $k$ converge to the corresponding cumulants of $X$ for all integers $k$ from $2$ up to a maximum order $m$. What is the value of $m$ required for this convergence?

Let $G \curvearrowright X$ be a free action of an amenable group on a compact metrizable space, satisfying the Uniform Rokhlin Property and comparison (URPC). A theorem establishes that the system is embeddable into the $M$-cubical shift $\left([0, 1]^M\right)^G$ provided that its mean dimension $\mathrm{mdim}(G \curvearrowright X)$ is strictly less than a critical threshold $T(M)$. Given that this bound recovers the known sharp shift embeddability theorem, what is the exact formula for the threshold $T(M)$?

Suppose a random field is constructed by assigning independent and identically distributed (IID) standardized random values to a Mercer/Karhunen-Loève deterministic orthonormal expansion in the Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) associated with a Matérn kernel $C$. Let the RKHS be the Sobolev space $W_2^m(\mathbb{R}^d)$ and assume all other Sobolev spaces are defined via scaling. There is a critical value $p_0$ such that the paths lie almost surely in the Sobolev space $W_2^p(\mathbb{R}^d)$ for all $p < p_0$, but almost surely not in $W_2^{p_0}(\mathbb{R}^d)$. What is the value of $p_0$ in terms of $m$ and $d$?

Let $n \ge 1$ be an integer. Let $(\mathscr{C},\mathbb{E},\mathfrak{s})$ be a Frobenius $n$-exangulated category. It has been proven that if $\mathscr{C}$ has Auslander-Reiten $n$-exangles, then its stable category $\overline{\mathscr{C}}$ has Auslander-Reiten $m$-angles for a specific integer $m$ depending on $n$. What is the value of $m$ in terms of $n$?

In the setting of binary error-correcting codes with noiseless feedback, a sender transmits a message over a noisy channel and, after sending each bit, learns exactly what was received and can tailor future bits accordingly. The unique decoding radius (i.e., the $1$-list decoding radius) for such codes is known to be $\frac{1}{3}$. What is the exact $2$-list decoding radius for binary error-correcting codes with noiseless feedback?

Consider the family of multi-dimensional continued fraction maps known as Triangle Partition (TRIP) maps. Let $p(n)$ denote the factor complexity of a sequence, where $n$ is the length of the factor. What is the proven upper bound on $p(n)$ for the S-adic sequences with rationally independent frequency vectors corresponding to the $(e,e,e)$-TRIP map (also known as the triangle map)?

Let $K$ be a differential field with field of constants $C$, and let $K(\partial_1,\ldots,\partial_k)$ be the left division ring of fractions of the ring of differential polynomials. It is proven that Hilbert's tenth problem over $K(\partial_1,\ldots,\partial_k)$ can be reduced to Hilbert's tenth problem over $C(t_1,\ldots,t_k)$. Let $k_{\mathbb{C}}$ be the minimum integer $k \ge 1$ for which this reduction explicitly yields a negative answer to Hilbert's tenth problem over $K(\partial_1,\ldots,\partial_k)$ when $C = \mathbb{C}$, and let $k_{\mathbb{R}}$ be the corresponding minimum integer $k \ge 1$ when $C = \mathbb{R}$. What is the ordered pair $(k_{\mathbb{C}}, k_{\mathbb{R}})$?

Consider simple graphs $G$ with a vertex-degree sequence $d_1, \dots, d_n$ satisfying $(d_1^p + \dots + d_n^p)^{1/p} \le C$, where $0 < p \le \infty$ and $C$ is a constant. The structure of the extremal graphs maximizing the number of $t$-cliques exhibits a dichotomy: depending on the value of $p$ relative to a critical value $p_0$, the asymptotically extremal construction is characterized by either a single clique or multiple cliques. What is this critical value $p_0$ as a function of $t$?

Let $\mathbb{X}$ be a set of $n \ge 7$ distinct points in the projective plane such that at least $n-3$ points among them lie on a line. The set of all possible values for the Waldschmidt constant of such a configuration $\mathbb{X}$ consists of exactly seven expressions. Six of these are $1$, $2$, $\frac{16}{7}$, $\frac{7}{3}$, $\frac{17}{7}$, and $\frac{5}{2}$. What is the seventh possible value, given as an expression in terms of $n$?

Consider a uniformly chosen integer-valued 1-Lipschitz function $f$ on a rooted $d$-ary tree of height $n$ (where each non-leaf vertex has exactly $d$ children), satisfying the boundary condition $f(v) = 0$ for all vertices $v$ at distance $n$ from the root. What is the maximum integer $d \ge 2$ such that the weak limit of the distribution of $f$ exists as $n \to \infty$?
For an odd prime $p$, what is the lower central width of the generalised Fabrykowski-Gupta groups and their profinite completions?

Calderón's splitting method establishes the global-in-time existence of weak solutions to the Navier-Stokes equations on $\mathbb{R}^3$ for supercritical divergence-free initial data in $L^p(\mathbb{R}^3)$ for $2 < p < 3$, blending the constructions of Leray and Giga-Kato. When generalizing this Calderón-like splitting to supercritical divergence-free Besov space initial data $u_0 \in \dot{B}^{s}_{q,\infty}(\mathbb{R}^3)$ with $q > 2$, the method successfully yields global-in-time weak solutions for $s$ in a specific open interval $(s_{\min}(q), s_{\max}(q))$ that fills the gap between Leray's $L^2$ weak solutions and the known mild solution theory in critical Besov spaces. What is the ordered pair $(s_{\min}(q), s_{\max}(q))$ as a function of $q$?
In the classification of special 2-flags up to local diffeomorphisms, what is the total number of singularity classes existing in length 5?

Let $ER_B(m)$ denote the bipartite Erdős-Rado number. The asymptotic growth rate of its logarithm is known to satisfy $\log ER_B(m) = \Theta(m^a (\log m)^b)$ for some real constants $a$ and $b$. What is the ordered pair $(a, b)$?

In the context of a modified Moran Birth-death process on a directed or undirected graph representing a spatial structure of population size $n$, consider the colonization setting where a single mutant individual invades an environment of residents. The residents have a reproduction rate of zero (meaning they never reproduce). In each step of this modified process, an individual is chosen uniformly at random from the population; if it is a mutant, it reproduces by replacing a uniformly random neighbor with a mutant, and if it is a resident, nothing happens, but the step is still counted. Across all possible spatial structures of size $n$, what is the exact maximum expected number of steps required for the mutation to completely colonize the population?

Let $\Omega$ be a connected infinite quiver. A theorem states that the category of locally finite-dimensional representations of $\Omega$ has unique representation type (meaning no two indecomposable representations have the same dimension vector) if and only if the underlying graph of $\Omega$ belongs to a certain set $S$ of infinite generalized ADE Dynkin diagrams. Up to isomorphism, what is the cardinality of the set $S$?

The basis number $b(G)$ of a graph $G$ is the smallest integer $k$ such that $G$ admits a basis for its cycle space in which each edge of $G$ belongs to at most $k$ members of the basis. What is the exact value of $b(G)$ for any non-planar graph $G$ that can be embedded on a surface with Euler characteristic $0$?

Let $\Gamma_n$ be the Cayley graph on the symmetric group $\mathrm{Sym}(n)$ whose connection set consists of all $n$-cycles in $\mathrm{Sym}(n)$. The energy of a graph, denoted by $\mathcal{E}(\Gamma_n)$, is defined as the sum of the absolute values of the eigenvalues of its adjacency matrix. For an integer $n \geq 4$, what is $\mathcal{E}(\Gamma_n)$ as a closed-form function of $n$?

When constructing tight-binding Hamiltonians for twisted bilayer graphene (TBG) under a commensurate perpendicular magnetic field, using linear vector potentials (such as the Landau gauge) causes the size of the tight-binding Hamiltonian to scale as $1/\theta^4$ as the twist angle $\theta \to 0$. When instead a general nonlinear vector potential is used, the Hamiltonian size can be reduced to match the minimal size dictated by flux quantization. In this case, the minimal size of the tight-binding Hamiltonian scales as $\theta^n$ as $\theta \to 0$. What is the value of the exponent $n$?

Assuming that the two-dimensional critical FK$_3$ percolation clusters converge in distribution to the CLE$_{24/5}$ gasket, what is the exact value of the bichromatic one-arm exponent for the critical 3-state Potts model?

Let $E_{k,n}^{\rm GG}$ be the Green-Griffiths bundles over the complex projective space $\mathbb{P}^N (\mathbb{C})$ with jet order $k$ and weight $n$. Let $\mathcal{O} (d)$ denote the degree $d$ twist of the tautological line bundle over $\mathbb{P}^N (\mathbb{C})$. For every integer $d \ge 1$ and for every jet order $k \ge d-1$, what is the exact value of the dimension
\[ \dim\, H^0 \bigg( \mathbb{P}^N (\mathbb{C}),\,\, \bigoplus_{n=1}^{\infty} E_{k, n}^{\text{GG}} \otimes \mathcal{O}(d) \bigg) \]
expressed as a function of $N$ and $d$?

A flexible embedded polyhedron is a closed polyhedral surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$ that can flex continuously without self-intersecting and without changing the shapes of its faces. What is the minimum possible number of vertices that such a polyhedron can have?

Let $N \ge 3$ be an odd integer. Consider the polynomial $p(z)=1+\kappa(z+z^{N-1})+z^N$ with a real parameter $\kappa$. What is the maximum value of $\kappa$ for which all the zeros of $p(z)$ lie on the unit circle?

Consider sets of $n > 0$ disjoint smooth rational curves on a K3 surface in characteristic 2 such that the sum of the curves is divisible by $2$ in the Picard group. While supersingular K3 surfaces can admit such sets for multiple values of $n$, what is the unique possible value of $n$ if the K3 surface has finite height?

Let $H$ be an $n$-vertex graph with minimum degree $\delta(H) = n/2 - \eta$. Assume that $\eta = \omega(1)$ and $\eta = o(n)$. It has been shown that there is a constant $C$ such that adding $(C+o(1))\eta$ uniformly random edges to $H$ is the sharp perturbed threshold for Hamiltonicity (meaning that adding $(C+o(1))\eta$ edges asymptotically almost surely makes any such graph $H$ Hamiltonian, and this is best possible up to the $o(1)$ error term). What is the value of the constant $C$?

Let $n$ and $s$ be integers such that $n > \max\{s, 162\}$ and $s \ge 1$. Let $f(n, s)$ be the minimum integer such that every $3$-partite $3$-uniform hypergraph $G$ with $n$ vertices in each of its $3$ vertex classes, having at least $f(n, s)$ edges, and containing no matching of size $s+1$, is guaranteed to have a vertex cover of size at most $s$. What is the closed-form expression for $f(n, s)$?

Let $n \geq 2$ be an integer and $I$ be a nonempty open real interval. Consider strictly monotone functions $f,g:I\to\mathbb{R}$ and vector-valued weight functions $p=(p_1,\dots,p_n):I\to\mathbb{R}_{+}^n$ and $q=(q_1,\dots,q_n):I\to\mathbb{R}_{+}^n$, all assumed to be first-order differentiable. Suppose that for all $(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in I^n$,
\[ f^{(-1)}\left(\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n p_i(x_i)f(x_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^n p_i(x_i)}\right)=g^{(-1)}\left(\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n q_i(x_i)g(x_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^n q_i(x_i)}\right), \]
where $f^{(-1)}$ and $g^{(-1)}$ denote the generalized left inverses of $f$ and $g$, respectively. This equality is proven to hold if and only if there exist four constants $a,b,c,d\in\mathbb{R}$ with $ad\neq bc$ such that $cf(x)+d>0$, $g(x) = \phi(f(x))$, and $q_\ell(x) = \psi(f(x), p_\ell(x))$ for all $x \in I$ and $\ell \in \{1,\dots,n\}$. Give the ordered pair $(\phi(f(x)), \psi(f(x), p_\ell(x)))$ in terms of $f(x)$, $p_\ell(x)$, and the constants $a,b,c,d$.
answer: \left( \frac{a f(x) + b}{c f(x) + d}, (c f(x) + d) p_\ell(x) \right)

question: Let $R(q)$ denote the Rogers-Ramanujan continued fraction. A modular identity expresses the quantity $\frac{1}{R(q^{2})R(q^{3})}+R(q^{2})R(q^{3})$ as a Laurent polynomial $P(x)$, where $x = \frac{R(q)}{R(q^{6})}$. What is $P(x)$?

Let $py(3, 2d)$ be the Pythagoras number of real ternary forms of degree $2d$, defined as the minimal integer $r$ such that every real ternary form of degree $2d$ which is a sum of squares of polynomials can be written as the sum of at most $r$ squares of real ternary forms of degree $d$. What is the exact value of the tuple $(py(3,8), py(3,10), py(3,12))$?

Consider the shelf shuffle on a deck of $n$ cards, which is a modified $2m$-handed riffle shuffle defined as follows: the deck is split multinomially into $2m$ piles, the even-numbered piles are reversed, and then cards are dropped from the piles proportionally to their sizes to form the shuffled deck. It is known that a cutoff phenomenon occurs in total variation distance with a constant window size as $n \to \infty$. What is the leading-order term of the cutoff time (i.e., the mixing time) as a function of $n$ and $m$?

A local antimagic labeling of a graph $F=(V,E)$ is a bijection $f: E \to \{1, \dots, |E|\}$ such that for any two adjacent vertices $u, v \in V$, their induced vertex weights $w(u) \neq w(v)$, where $w(u) = \sum_{e \in E(u)} f(e)$ and $E(u)$ is the set of edges incident to $u$. The local antimagic chromatic number of $F$, denoted $\chi_{la}(F)$, is the minimum number of distinct vertex weights over all local antimagic labelings of $F$. Let $G$ be a 1-regular graph and $H$ be a null graph (a graph with at least one vertex and no edges). What is the exact value of $\chi_{la}(G \vee H)$, where $G \vee H$ denotes the join of $G$ and $H$?

In the random $2$-SAT model, the limiting empirical marginal distribution is purely discrete for all clause-to-variable densities up to a critical value $\alpha_c$, and possesses a nontrivial continuous part for any density in the interval $(\alpha_c, 1)$. What is the exact value of $\alpha_c$?

For any finitely generated group $G$, let $S_G$ be the set of integers $n \geq 1$ such that $G$ admits a finite quotient of order $n$. A theorem establishes that the natural density of $S_G$ within the positive integers always exists and can only take values in a specific set of three numbers. What is this set of possible values for the natural density?

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ such that all coordinates of all points in $S$ belong to $\frac{1}{2}\mathbb{Z}$, and for any two distinct points $x, y \in S$, the Manhattan distance $\|x - y\|_1$ is an odd integer. As a function of $n$, what is the maximum possible cardinality of $S$?

For $\tau>0$, let $S(\tau)$ denote the set of $(x_1,x_2)\in \mathbb{R}^2$ simultaneously satisfying the inequalities $\|q x_1 \| < q^{-\tau}$ and $\|q x_2 \| < e^{-q}$ for infinitely many $q \in \mathbb{N}$, where $\| x \|$ denotes the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer. What is the exact value of the Hausdorff dimension of $S(\tau)$?

Let $d \ge 1$ and $s \le 2^d$ be nonnegative integers. For a subset $A$ of vertices of the $n$-dimensional hypercube $Q_n$ (where $n \ge d$), let $\lambda(n,d,s,A)$ denote the fraction of $d$-dimensional subcubes $Q_d$ of $Q_n$ that contain exactly $s$ vertices of $A$. Let $\lambda(n,d,s)$ denote the maximum possible value of $\lambda(n,d,s,A)$ as $A$ ranges over all subsets of vertices of $Q_n$, and let $\lambda(d,s)$ denote the limit of $\lambda(n,d,s)$ as $n$ tends to infinity. What is the sum of all values of $s$ (as a function of $d$) for which $\lambda(d,s) = 1$?

Let $P_{2n}(x)$ be a random polynomial of degree $2n$ with coefficients given by an i.i.d. sequence of mean-zero, variance-1 random variables, multiplied by an $\frac{\alpha}{2}$-regularly varying sequence for $\alpha>-1$. As $n \to \infty$, the probability that $P_{2n}(x)$ has no real zeros is asymptotically $n^{-\gamma}$. Let $b_{\lambda}$ denote the persistence exponent of a mean-zero, one-dimensional stationary Gaussian process with covariance function $\mathrm{sech}(\tau/2)^{\lambda+1}$. What is the exact value of $\gamma$ expressed in terms of $b_{\alpha}$ and $b_0$?

Let $F_5 = \{abc, abd, cde\}$ denote the generalized triangle. What is the optimal constant $c$ such that for all $n \ge 5000$, every $n$-vertex $F_5$-free $3$-uniform hypergraph with minimum degree greater than $c n^2$ is guaranteed to be $3$-partite?

For a bounded measurable set $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $\Phi(A)$ denote the two-dimensional Lebesgue measure of the set $\{(x, y)\in A^2\colon x\le y\le x+1\}$. Let $I$ be an interval of length $L$, and suppose $I = A_1\cup\dots\cup A_{k+1}$ partitions $I$ into $k+1$ measurable pieces. What is the optimal multiplicative constant $c_k$, depending only on $k$, such that the inequality $\sum_{i=1}^{k+1} \Phi(A_i)\ge c_k L - 1$ holds for all such intervals and partitions?

Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges, and let $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n$ be the degrees of its vertices. Let $\rho(G)$ be the spectral radius of $G$, and let the degree deviation of $G$ be defined as $S = \sum_{i=1}^n \left|d_i - \frac{2m}{n}\right|$. It was originally conjectured by Nikiforov (2006) for sufficiently large $m$ and $n$, and later proven for all $m$ and $n$, that $\rho(G) - \frac{2m}{n}$ is bounded above by a tight function of $S$. What is this tight upper bound on $\rho(G) - \frac{2m}{n}$ expressed in terms of $S$?

Let $S = \mathrm{k}[x_1,\ldots,x_n]$ be a polynomial ring over a field $\mathrm{k}$ with the standard grading, and let $I$ be an arbitrary nonzero squarefree monomial ideal of $S$ such that the Krull dimension of $S/I$ is $d$. Let $\mu$ be the number of associated primes of $S/I$ of dimension $d$. What is the multiplicity $e_0(S/I^s)$ of $S/I^s$ for any integer $s \ge 1$, as a closed-form function of $\mu$, $n$, $d$, and $s$?

Let $V$ be a vector space over a finite field $\mathrm{GF}(q)$ of characteristic not $2$, equipped with a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form. Let $G$ be the commutator subgroup of the associated orthogonal group $O(V)$. An element of $G$ is called reversible if it is conjugate to its inverse in $G$, and it is called a bireflection if it can be written as the product of two involutions in $G$. It is a theorem that every reversible element of $G$ is a bireflection, except in exactly one scenario where $V$ is a hyperbolic space. This exception occurs exactly when $(q \bmod 4, \dim V \bmod 4)$ is equal to a specific ordered pair $(a, b)$ with $0 \le a, b < 4$. What is the ordered pair $(a, b)$?

Let $S \subset \mathbb{R}^3$ be an asymptotically flat support surface with nonnegative mean curvature, and let $D$ be its outermost free boundary minimal surface. Let $m$ be the exterior mass of $S$. The extrinsic Penrose inequality establishes a sharp lower bound for $m$ in terms of the area $|D|$ of $D$ (with equality holding if the unbounded component of $S \setminus \partial D$ is a half-catenoid). What is this lower bound for $m$?

Let $p > 37$ be a prime. What is the tight lower bound on the number of conjugacy classes of non-identity proper subgroups of $\mathrm{PSL}_2(p)$ (which is known to be attained for over a million primes)?

The spread of a graph is the difference between the largest and smallest eigenvalue of its adjacency matrix. For a fixed integer $r \ge 3$ and sufficiently large $n$, what is the maximum spread of an $n$-vertex graph that does not contain $K_r$ as a minor? Provide your answer as a closed-form expression in terms of $n$ and $r$.

The $\sigma_t$-irregularity of a graph $G$ is defined as $\sigma_t(G) = \sum_{\{u,v\}\subseteq V(G)} (d(u)-d(v))^2$, where $d(z)$ denotes the degree of vertex $z$. Among all complete bipartite graphs on $n$ vertices, the maximum $\sigma_t$-irregularity is achieved by $K_{a, n-a}$. For $n$ sufficiently large, the value of the smaller partition size $a \le n/2$ that maximizes $\sigma_t(K_{a,n-a})$ is given by $\lfloor c n \rfloor$ or $\lceil c n \rceil$ for some constant $c \in (0, 1/2)$. What is the exact value of $c$?

In the study of the fractional Schrödinger equation on rank-one symmetric spaces of non-compact type $\mathbb{X}$, it is established that the solution $u(x,t)$ converges pointwise almost everywhere to the initial radial data $f \in H^s(\mathbb{X})$ with compact support as time $t$ approaches $0$, provided that $s > c$. Extending Sjölin's results to this setting, what is the critical lower bound $c$ for the Sobolev regularity index?

Let $\mathrm{T}_{r} = \left\{\{1,\ldots, r-1,r\}, \{1,\ldots, r-1,r+1\},\{r,r+1, \ldots, 2r-1\}\right\}$ be the $r$-uniform generalized triangle. For an $n$-vertex $r$-uniform hypergraph $\mathcal{H}$, the positive codegree of an $(r-1)$-tuple of vertices is defined as the number of edges containing it, provided it is contained in at least one edge. It is proven that for every $n \ge (r-1)(2r+1)/2$, if $\mathcal{H}$ is an $n$-vertex $\mathrm{T}_{r}$-free $r$-uniform hypergraph in which every $(r-1)$-tuple of vertices has a positive codegree strictly greater than $f(n,r)$, then $\mathcal{H}$ is $r$-partite. What is the tight threshold $f(n,r)$?

Let $\mathcal{O}_q(\mathrm{F}_3)$ be the full quantum flag manifold of $\mathcal{O}_q(\mathrm{SU}_3)$. A quantum tangent space and an associated $*$-differential calculus are constructed for this manifold using Lusztig's positive root vectors (with respect to a distinguished reduced decomposition of the longest element of the Weyl group). Within the noncommutative complex geometry of this calculus, what is the exact number of left $\mathcal{O}_q(\mathrm{SU}_3)$-covariant almost-complex structures?

In the semidiscrete setting of optimal transport, where the input probability measure is supported on a compact convex set with nonempty interior and has a strictly positive Lipschitz continuous Lebesgue density, and the output measure is finitely discrete, the classical problem is approximated by entropic optimal transport with a regularization parameter $\varepsilon > 0$. The approximation rate of the entropic optimal transport map (in approaching the Brenier map) as $\varepsilon \to 0$ under the dual Lipschitz norm is known to be sharp and given by $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^p)$ up to polylogarithmic factors. What is the value of the exponent $p$?

Let $a_1$ and $a_2$ be fixed positive integers. Let $G$ be the 4-regular circulant graph of prime order $p$ with generators $a_1$ and $a_2$. For sufficiently large $p$, what is the maximum multiplicity of the eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix of $G$?

Consider $n$ people sitting around a circular table, where $n$ is an even positive integer. Each person randomly and independently strictly prefers either their left neighbor or their right neighbor, with probability $1/2$ for each choice. Assume everyone prefers being matched with either neighbor to being unmatched. A stable matching of these $n$ people is a matching of adjacent individuals such that no two neighbors mutually prefer each other to their current status in the matching. As a function of $n$, what is the probability that there exists a stable matching in which every person is matched?

Let $\phi$ denote Euler's totient function. For any given constants $a \in \mathbb{N}$, $b \in \mathbb{Z}$, and $c, d \in \mathbb{R}$ with $c > 0$, the set of fractions $\frac{\phi(an+b)}{cn+d}$ evaluated at integers $n$ (for $n$ large enough such that $an+b > 0$ and $cn+d \neq 0$) lies dense in a maximal interval $(0, D]$. What is the exact value of $D$ in terms of $a$, $b$, and $c$?

For certain classes of polynomials $P(x)$ satisfying specific Diophantine conditions on the coefficient of $x$, it is known that the asymptotic density of the set of natural numbers $n$ such that $\gcd(n, \lfloor P(n) \rfloor) = 1$ exists and is a constant independent of $P$. What is the exact value of this density?

Let $f \colon \mathbb{R}^d 	o \mathbb{R}$ be a $C^2$ function with a unique global minimizer $x^*$. Assume the Polyak-Lojasiewicz constant $C_{	ext{PL}}(f)$ is finite, and there exists $L > 0$ such that $\Delta f \le L(1 + \|
abla f\|^2)$. For $t > 0$, let $\mu_t$ be the probability measure on $\mathbb{R}^d$ with density proportional to $\exp(-f(x)/t)$, and let $C_{\mathsf{P}}(\mu_t)$ be its Poincaré constant, defined as the smallest constant $C$ such that $\mathrm{Var}_{\mu_t}(g) \le C \int \|
abla g\|^2 \, d\mu_t$ for all locally Lipschitz functions $g$. Let $H = 
abla^2 f(x^*)$ be the Hessian of $f$ at the minimizer. What is the value of the low-temperature limit $\lim_{t	o 0^+} t^{-1} C_{\mathsf{P}}(\mu_t)$? Express your answer as a closed-form expression in terms of $H$, using $\lambda_{\min}(\cdot)$ to denote the smallest eigenvalue of a real symmetric matrix.

A graph $G$ is called $(c,t)$-sparse if for every pair of vertex subsets $A,B\subset V(G)$ with $|A|,|B|\geq t$, the number of edges between them satisfies $e(A,B)\leq (1-c)|A||B|$. Fox, Nenadov, and Pham conjectured that for every $c>0$ and integer $\ell \ge 2$, there exists a constant $C>1$ such that if an $n$-vertex graph $G$ is $(c,t)$-sparse for some $t$, and has at least $C t^\alpha n^{1+1/\ell}$ edges, then $G$ contains an induced copy of the cycle $C_{2\ell}$. What is the exponent $\alpha$ as a function of $\ell$?

Consider the family $\mathcal{E}$ of representatives for the $\mathbb{Q}$-isomorphism classes of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$, consisting of curves $E_{A,B}: y^2 = x^3 + Ax + B$ with $A, B \in \mathbb{Z}$, $4A^3+27B^2 \neq 0$, and no prime $p$ such that $p^4|A$ and $p^6|B$. When the curves in $\mathcal{E}$ with complex multiplication are ordered by their naive height $h^{\text{naive}}(E_{A,B}) = \max(4|A|^3, 27|B|^2)$, asymptotically $100\%$ of them have complex multiplication by an order of discriminant $D$. What is the value of $D$?

In the context of measurement-constrained M-estimation for estimating a high-dimensional parameter $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ with sparsity $s$ in a linear threshold $\theta^T Z$ for a continuous variable $X$ predicting a binary outcome $Y$, a $K$-step active subsampling algorithm is used to iteratively sample informative observations to label, subject to a total label budget $N$. The theoretical convergence rate of the resulting regularized M-estimator exhibits a phase transition with respect to the smoothness parameter $\beta \geq 1$ of the conditional density of $X$ given $Y$ and $Z$. There is a critical value $\beta_c$ such that if $\beta > \beta_c$, a two-step algorithm ($K=2$) yields an estimator with the parametric convergence rate $O_p((s \log d / N)^{1/2})$ in the $\ell_2$ norm. However, for $1 < \beta \leq \beta_c$, the two-step algorithm is sub-optimal and requires $K>2$ steps to attain the same parametric rate. What is the exact value of the critical smoothness threshold $\beta_c$?

Let $k \geq 2$ be an integer. The canonical Ramsey theorem of Erdős and Rado implies that any edge-colouring of the complete graph $K_n$ (for $n$ sufficiently large) contains a copy of the even cycle $C_{2k}$ that is monochromatic, rainbow, or lexicographic. For the binomial random graph $G(n,p)$, the threshold probability for the property that asymptotically almost surely any edge-colouring induces a canonical copy of $C_{2k}$ is determined, up to a logarithmic factor, by $p = \omega(n^{-x} \log n)$. What is the exact value of the exponent $x$ as a function of $k$?

Fix an integer $s \ge 2$. Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a set of $n$ points and let $\mathcal{L}$ be a set of lines in a linear space such that no line in $\mathcal{L}$ contains more than $\frac{n-1}{s-1}$ points of $\mathcal{P}$. Suppose that for every $s$-element subset $S$ of $\mathcal{P}$, there is a pair of points in $S$ that both lie in some single line from $\mathcal{L}$. For sufficiently large $n$ such that $n-1$ is a multiple of $s-1$, what is the exact minimum possible number of lines $|\mathcal{L}|$ as a function of $n$ and $s$?

Let $k$ be an algebraically closed field of characteristic zero. Let $\mathfrak{D}=k[x_0,x_1,x_2]/(2x_0x_2-x_1^2-1)$ be a ring on which $\mathbb{Z}_2$ acts by $x_i\to -x_i$ for $i \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. Let $\mathfrak{R}=\mathfrak{D}^{\mathbb{Z}_2}$ be the subring of invariants, and let $X={\rm Spec}(\mathfrak{R})$. What is the minimal integer $n$ such that the surface $X$ admits an algebraic embedding in the affine space $\mathbb{A}_k^n$?

Consider the boundary $\partial \mathbb D$ of the free Brownian disk $\mathbb D$ as a metric space by endowing it with the restriction of the metric of $\mathbb D$. Almost surely, the uniform measure on $\partial \mathbb D$ coincides with $\kappa$ times the Hausdorff measure associated with a gauge function of the form $h(s) = s^\alpha (\log\log(1/s))^\beta$ for some deterministic constant $\kappa>0$ and real constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$. What is the ordered pair $(\alpha, \beta)$?

A sequence covering array, denoted $\textsf{SCA}(N;t,v)$, is a set of $N$ permutations of $\{0, \dots, v-1 \}$ such that each sequence of $t$ distinct elements of $\{0, \dots, v-1\}$ reads left to right in at least one permutation. The minimum number of permutations such an array can have is $t!$. Levenshtein conjectured that if an $\textsf{SCA}(t!;t,v)$ exists, then $v \in \{t,t+1\}$. A strength 2 excess coverage array, denoted $\textsf{CA}_{X}(N;2,k,v)$, is an $N \times k$ array with entries from $\{0, \dots, v - 1\}$ such that every ordered pair of distinct symbols appear at least once in each pair of columns and all other pairs appear at least twice. By demonstrating that there is a unique $\textsf{CA}_{X}(42;2,5,6)$ and showing it does not satisfy necessary conditions, a recent theorem established that if an $\textsf{SCA}(7!;7,v)$ exists, then $v \le V$ for a specific integer upper bound $V$. What is the value of $V$?

Consider the energy per particle of a two-dimensional lattice $\Lambda \subset \mathbb{C}$ given by $E_\alpha(\Lambda) = \sum_{P \in \Lambda} |P|^4 e^{-\pi \alpha |P|^2}$, where $\alpha > 0$. Any two-dimensional lattice of unit density (where the fundamental domain has area 1) can be parameterized, up to rotation, as $\Lambda_\tau = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\text{Im}(\tau)}} (\mathbb{Z} + \tau \mathbb{Z})$ for some $\tau$ in the standard fundamental domain $\mathcal{F} = \{\tau \in \mathbb{C} : \text{Im}(\tau) > 0, |\tau| \geq 1, -1/2 < \text{Re}(\tau) \leq 1/2\}$. For $\alpha \geq \frac{3}{2}$, it has been proven that the minimum of $E_\alpha(\Lambda_\tau)$ over all such unit-density lattices is attained at a unique $\tau \in \mathcal{F}$. What is this value of $\tau$? Express your answer in the form $e^{i\theta}$ for a real number $\theta$.

Let $G$ be an $n$-vertex graph. A proper edge-colouring of $G$ is called globally $cn$-bounded if every colour is assigned to at most $cn$ edges. Consider the following statement: for any $\varepsilon > 0$, every globally $cn$-bounded properly edge-coloured graph $G$ with minimum degree $\delta(G) \geq (1/2+\varepsilon)n$ contains a Hamilton cycle with $n-o(n)$ distinct colours as $n \to \infty$. What is the supremum of the set of real numbers $c > 0$ for which this statement is true?

Let $\tau(n,t)$ be the smallest integer such that every $n \times n \times n$ tripartite graph with minimum degree $n + \tau(n,t)$ contains a copy of the complete tripartite graph $K_{t, t, t}$. It is proven that $\tau(n,t) = \mathcal{O}(n^{\alpha(t)})$, and that this bound is optimal up to a constant factor under the assumption that the Turán number satisfies $\operatorname{ex}(n, K_{t, t}) = \Theta(n^{2 - 1/t})$. What is the exact expression for $\alpha(t)$?

The Karp--Sipser algorithm consists in recursively removing all leaves along with their unique neighbors and all isolated vertices of a given graph. The remaining graph obtained when there is no leaf left is called the Karp--Sipser core. Consider the sparse Erdős--Rényi random graph $G(n, \lambda/n)$. It is known that the size of its Karp--Sipser core exhibits a phase transition at $\lambda=\mathrm{e}$. At this critical value $\lambda=\mathrm{e}$, the size of the Karp--Sipser core is of order $n^\alpha$. What is the exact value of the exponent $\alpha$?

Let $G$ be a simple $d$-regular graph on $n$ vertices. Given an integer $k \ge 1$, an edge-$k$-coloring of $G$ is an assignment of colors $1, \dots, k$ to the edges of $G$ such that no two adjacent edges receive the same color. A sum-distinguishing edge-$k$-coloring of $G$ is an edge-$k$-coloring such that for any two distinct vertices $u$ and $v$, the sum of the colors on the edges incident with $u$ is different from the sum of the colors on the edges incident with $v$. The sum-distinguishing chromatic index, denoted $\chi'_{sd}(G)$, is the smallest integer $k$ such that $G$ has a sum-distinguishing edge-$k$-coloring. For $n$ even and sufficiently large, and $d \ge \frac{2n}{3}$, what is the exact value of $\chi'_{sd}(G)$ expressed as a function of $d$?

Permutation clones on a set $A$ are families of permutations of $A^n$ (for $n \ge 1$) that generalize clone theory. It is known that the dual structure to a permutation clone consists of weight mappings $A^k\rightarrow M$ to a commutative monoid $M$. A permutation clone is called *relationally defined* if it corresponds to the case where the dual objects are relations, which is equivalent to the case where $M=\mathbb{B}$. What is the exact number of relationally defined permutation clones on a $2$-element set?

Let $X$ be a nonsingular projective 3-fold of general type with third plurigenus $P_3 \geq 2$, and let $\phi_m$ denote the $m$-th pluricanonical map of $X$. What is the smallest integer $m_0$ such that $\phi_m$ is guaranteed to be birational onto its image for all $m \geq m_0$ on all such 3-folds $X$?

Let $d \geq 3$ and $k \geq 2$ be integers. It is a theorem that the set of limit points of the first $k$ eigenvalues (sorted in descending order) of sequences of $d$-regular graphs is exactly the set of tuples $(\mu_1,\dots,\mu_k)$ satisfying $d=\mu_1\geq \mu_2 \geq \dots\geq \mu_{k}\geq B(d)$ for some function $B(d)$. What is the closed-form expression for $B(d)$?
Let $G$ be a directed strongly regular graph with parameters $(v, k, t, \lambda, \mu) = (22, 9, 6, 3, 4)$. What is the maximum possible prime order of an automorphism of $G$?

A set of edges $F$ in a graph $G$ is an edge dominating set if every edge in $G$ is either in $F$ or shares a vertex with an edge in $F$. A graph $G$ is said to be well-edge-dominated if all of its minimal edge dominating sets have the same cardinality. Up to isomorphism, how many 2-connected well-edge-dominated outerplanar graphs are there?

Let $M$ be a smooth complete non-compact Riemannian manifold without boundary of dimension $n\geq 2$ with at least two ends. It has been proven that if the principal eigenvalue $\lambda_1(-\gamma\Delta+\mathrm{Ric})\geq 0$ for some $\gamma < c_n$, then $M$ splits isometrically as $\mathbb R\times N$ for some compact manifold $N$ with nonnegative Ricci curvature, and that the constant $c_n$ is sharp (the splitting may not hold if $\gamma \geq c_n$). What is the exact value of the sharp constant $c_n$ as a function of $n$?

Let $d=d(n,p)$ be the maximum dimension for which an Erdős-Rényi $G(n,p)$ random graph is $d$-rigid. There are two different regimes of rigidity in $G(n,p)$ separated at a critical probability $p_c=C_*\frac{\log n}{n}$, where $\log$ denotes the natural logarithm. It is known that if $p < (1-\varepsilon)p_c$, then $d(n,p)$ is asymptotically almost surely (a.a.s.) equal to the minimum degree of $G(n,p)$, whereas if $p_c \leq p = o(n^{-1/2})$, then $d(n,p)$ is a.a.s. equal to $(1/2 + o(1))np$. What is the exact value of the constant $C_*$?
