Let $\phi_3(n)$ denote the minimum size of a family $\mathcal{E}$ of $3$-element subsets of $V$ such that for every $3$-partition $X, Y, Z$ of $V$, there exists some $\{x,y,z\} \in \mathcal{E}$ with $x \in X$, $y \in Y$, and $z \in Z$. What is the exact value of $\phi_3(n)$ as a function of $n$?

Consider solutions $u(t,x) \in L^\infty_t B_{2,1}^{\frac{1}{2}}$ to the Benjamin-Ono equation $\partial_t u - H \partial_x^2 u = -\partial_x(u^2)$ that are localized in a reference frame moving to the right with constant speed $c>0$. It is a known result that if such a solution decays spatially at least as fast as $\langle x-ct\rangle^{-1-\epsilon}$ for some $\epsilon>0$, then it must in fact decay as $\langle x-ct\rangle^{-\alpha}$. Assuming $\alpha$ is the sharpest possible exponent, what is the value of $\alpha$?

The colouring defect of a cubic graph $G$ is the smallest number of edges left uncovered by any three perfect matchings of $G$. A cycle cover of $G$ is a collection of cycles in $G$ such that every edge of $G$ is contained in at least one cycle. The length of a cycle cover is the sum of the lengths of the cycles it contains. Let $c(G)$ be the minimum length of a cycle cover of $G$. 

What is the exact maximum value of $c(G)$, expressed as a function of the number of edges $m$, among all cubic graphs $G$ with $m$ edges that have a colouring defect of $3$?

Let $\mathbb{R}^2$ be equipped with the standard Euclidean metric, and let $\mathbb{Z}^2$ be equipped with the induced Euclidean metric. What is the exact Gromov-Hausdorff distance $d_{GH}(\mathbb{R}^2, \mathbb{Z}^2)$?

Let $S$ and $T$ be finite symmetric sets of integers containing $1$. For a set of integers $C$, let $\Sigma_C$ denote the set of all complex roots of power series whose coefficients are restricted to lie in $C$. Suppose that $\Sigma_S = \Sigma_T$. It has been proven that $S$ and $T$ must agree on all elements between $1$ and a specific upper bound $B$ (i.e., $S \cap [1, B) = T \cap [1, B)$). What is the exact expression for this upper bound $B$ in terms of $M = \max(S)$?

Let $n > 2$ and $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$. It has been proven that if two smooth $n$-manifolds $M$ and $N$ each admit a complete Riemannian metric satisfying $-\gamma\Delta + \mathrm{Ric} > \lambda$, then their connected sum $M \# N$ also admits such a metric, provided that $\gamma$ is strictly greater than a sharp lower bound $\gamma_{\text{min}}(n)$. What is this sharp lower bound $\gamma_{\text{min}}(n)$?

Let $\Omega^c$ denote the $c$-affine surface area for convex bodies in $\mathbb{R}^n$. It has been proven that on the class of $n$-dimensional ball-bodies (defined as intersections of translates of the unit Euclidean ball), $\Omega^c$ is maximized by a Euclidean ball. What is the radius of this maximizing ball as a function of the dimension $n$?

A cohomology class $u$ of a topological space $X$ is called atoroidal if its pullback to the torus vanishes for every continuous map from a torus to $X$. A space $X$ is atoroidally symplectic if there is a field $F$ of characteristic $0$ or $>2n$, and an atoroidal cohomology class $u \in H^2(X;F)$ such that the cup product $u^n$ is non-zero. Let $X$ be a connected atoroidally symplectic CW-complex of dimension $2n$. What is the topological complexity of $X$?

The conventional Robertson preparation uncertainty relation bounds the product of the variances of two observables $A$ and $B$ evaluated on a quantum state with density operator $ho$ by $\frac{1}{4} |\langle [A,B] \rangle|^2$. Assuming $\rho$ is not a maximally mixed state, by using spectral properties of $\rho$, this relation can be strictly generalized by replacing the constant $1/4$ with a state-dependent coefficient $C(\lambda_{\max}, \lambda_{\min})$ that depends only on the largest ($\lambda_{\max}$) and smallest ($\lambda_{\min}$) eigenvalues of $\rho$. What is the explicit formula for the optimal coefficient $C(\lambda_{\max}, \lambda_{\min})$ that provides the tightest possible Robertson-type bound of the form $\Delta A^2 \Delta B^2 \ge C(\lambda_{\max}, \lambda_{\min}) |\langle [A,B] \rangle|^2$?

Let the sequence $a(n)$ be defined by the generating function $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a(n) q^n = \frac{(-q; q)^2_\infty}{(q; q)_\infty}$ for $|q|<1$, where $(a; q)_\infty = \prod_{k=0}^\infty (1-a q^k)$ is the $q$-Pochhammer symbol. There exists a unique integer $b \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ such that $a(7n+b) \equiv 0 \pmod{7}$ for all integers $n \ge 0$. What is the value of $b$?

Let $G$ be a simple graph. The independence polynomial of $G$ is defined as $I_G(z)=\sum_{i=0}^\alpha a_i z^i$, where $a_i$ is the number of independent sets in $G$ with cardinality $i$ and $\alpha$ is the independence number of $G$. Let $G^m$ denote the $m$-times lexicographic product of $G$ with itself. The independence attractor of $G$, denoted by $\mathcal{A}(G)$, is defined as $\mathcal{A}(G) = \lim_{m\rightarrow \infty} \{z: I_{G^m}(z)=0\}$, where the limit is taken with respect to the Hausdorff metric on the space of all compact subsets of the complex plane. If $\mathcal{A}(G)$ is a line segment, it has been proven that $\mathcal{A}(G)$ must be of the form $\left[-\frac{4}{k}, 0\right]$ for $k$ belonging to a specific finite set of positive integers $S$. What is the set $S$?

Let $G$ be a graph. A distinguishing 2-coloring of $G$ is a partition of $V(G)$ into 2 color classes such that no non-identity automorphism of $G$ preserves the color classes. A graph is 2-distinguishable if it admits a distinguishing 2-coloring. For a 2-distinguishable graph $G$, the cost $ho(G)$ is defined as the minimum size of a color class over all distinguishing 2-colorings of $G$. The determining number $Det(G)$ is the minimum size of a subset of vertices $S \subseteq V(G)$ such that the only automorphism of $G$ that fixes every vertex in $S$ pointwise is the identity automorphism. What is the minimal set of integers $X$ for which it is proven that $\rho(G) \in X$ for any 2-distinguishable graph $G$ that has $Det(G) = 2$?

For a positive integer $n \geq 3$, an $n$-tuple of independent dice (i.e., independent discrete uniform random variables with finite support in the positive integers) $(A_1,A_2,\dots,A_n)$ is called balanced if $P(A_1<A_2) = P(A_2<A_3) = \cdots = P(A_n<A_1)$ and nontransitive if these probabilities are each strictly greater than $\frac{1}{2}$. What is the supremum of the winning probability $P(A_1 < A_2)$ over all balanced and nontransitive $n$-tuples of dice, as a function of $n$?

Let $g_1, g_2$ be loxodromic isometries of the complex hyperbolic plane $\mathbb{H}^2_{\mathbb{C}}$. Consider the cyclic subgroup $\Gamma = \langle (g_1, g_2) \rangle$ acting on the complex hyperbolic bidisk $\mathbb{H}^2_{\mathbb{C}} \times \mathbb{H}^2_{\mathbb{C}}$. Suppose the basepoint for the Dirichlet domain is chosen as $(z_1, z_2)$, where $z_1$ and $z_2$ are points lying on the invariant axes of $g_1$ and $g_2$, respectively. How many sides does the Dirichlet domain of $\Gamma$ have?

Let $B_k$ denote the Bernoulli numbers (with $B_1 = -1/2$), $s(n,k)$ denote the signed Stirling numbers of the first kind, and $b(n,k)$ denote the Bessel numbers of the first kind. There exists an identity linking these numbers of the form:
\[ \sum_{k=0}^{n} b(n,k)\,\frac{(-1)^k\,k!}{k+1} = \sum_{k=0}^{n} c_{n,k}\,s(n,k)\,B_k \]
What is the closed-form expression for $c_{n,k}$?

Consider the 2D Boussinesq equations around Couette flow and large affine temperature on the domain $\mathbb{T} \times \mathbb{R}$, with equal kinematic viscosity and thermal diffusivity ($\nu = \kappa$). What is the exact numerical value of the Sobolev stability threshold proven for this system?

Let $K$ be a compact set in a complex manifold $\mathcal{M}$. The $q$-nucleus of $K$, denoted by $N_q(K)$, is defined as the maximal $q$-pseudoconcave subset of $K$ (i.e., the subset of $K$ containing all other compact $q$-pseudoconcave subsets in $K$). According to the characterization of such compact sets, there exists a $q$-convex function with corners in a neighborhood of $K$ if and only if $N_q(K)$ is equal to what set?
What is the total number of isomorphism classes of smooth weak Fano toric fourfolds of Picard rank $3$?

Let $S$ be a Damek-Ricci space and $\mathcal{L}$ be either the Laplace-Beltrami or the shifted Laplace-Beltrami operator. Consider the dispersive equation $i\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + \Psi(\sqrt{-\mathcal{L}})u = 0$ on $S \times \mathbb{R}$ with radial initial data $u(0,\cdot) = f$. Suppose the corresponding phase function $\psi(\lambda)$ satisfies the large frequency asymptotic $\psi(\lambda) = \lambda^a + \mathcal{O}(1)$ for $\lambda \gg 1$, where $a \in (0,1)$. What is the exact expression for the lower bound $\beta_0$ (in terms of $a$) of the almost sharp regularity threshold $\beta > \beta_0$ established for the almost everywhere pointwise convergence of the solution $u$ to $f$?

Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$ with girth $g \ge 4$. For an integer $k$ such that $1 \le k \le \min\{g-1, n-g\}$, let $n(G,k)$ be the number of Laplacian eigenvalues (counting multiplicities) of $G$ that fall inside the interval $[n-g-k+4, n]$. What is the tight upper bound on $n(G,k)$ valid for all such graphs, expressed in terms of $n$ and $g$?

Let $T$ be a tree that is not an edge. It is a proven theorem that for almost every graph $G$ which does not contain $T$ as an induced subgraph, the vertex set $V(G)$ has a partition into $k$ parts, where each part induces a $P_4$-free graph. What is the value of $k$ as a function of the independence number $\alpha(T)$ of $T$?

A family $\mathcal{F}$ of graphs is asymptotically $\chi$-bounded with bounding function $f$ if almost every graph $G \in \mathcal{F}$ satisfies $\chi(G) \le f(\omega(G))$, where $\chi(G)$ is the chromatic number and $\omega(G)$ is the clique number. For the hereditary family of $C_6$-free graphs (graphs not containing the 6-vertex cycle $C_6$ as an induced subgraph), the bounding function is known to be of the form $f(w) = (1+o(1))g(w)$ as $w \to \infty$. What is the function $g(w)$?

Let $C_d$ be the typical cell of a homogeneous Poisson-Voronoi tessellation in $\mathbb{R}^d$. Let $N_{d,k}$ denote the number of faces of dimension $d-k$ of $C_d$. It is established that for a fixed integer $k \ge 1$, as $d \to \infty$, the sequence of random variables $N_{d,k}^{1/d}$ converges in probability to a constant $c_k$. What is $c_k$ as a function of $k$?

Let $x_\theta$ and $y_\theta$ be the coordinate functions of the generalized Heighway dragon curve associated with the angle $\theta$, where $\frac{\pi}{3} < \theta < \frac{5\pi}{3}$. What is the exact box-counting dimension of the graph of these coordinate functions, expressed as a function of $\theta$?

Let $S$ be the set of all finite cubic graphs whose adjacency matrix has no eigenvalues in the open interval $(-2,0)$. The set $S$ consists of exactly one infinite family $\mathcal{F}$ (containing exactly one graph on $6n$ vertices for each integer $n \ge 2$) and a finite set $\mathcal{E}$ of exceptional graphs. What is the cardinality $|\mathcal{E}|$?

Let $A$ be a strictly positive $n \times m$ matrix, and let $\mathbf{r} \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}^n$ and $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}^m$ be target row and column sum vectors such that $\sum_{i=1}^n r_i = \sum_{j=1}^m c_j$. The generalized Sinkhorn limit is the unique matrix $B$ of the form $B = D_1 A D_2$, where $D_1$ and $D_2$ are diagonal matrices with positive entries, such that the row sums of $B$ are $\mathbf{r}$ and the column sums of $B$ are $\mathbf{c}$. Each entry of $B$ is algebraic over the field generated by the entries of $A$, $\mathbf{r}$, and $\mathbf{c}$. What is the maximum algebraic degree of the entries of $B$ over this field for generic inputs $A$, $\mathbf{r}$, and $\mathbf{c}$, as a function of $n$ and $m$?

Let $t \in (0,1)$. According to the Balogh--Kemkes--Lee--Young conjecture for the tetrahedron case, for any $\mu>0$ and sufficiently large $n \in 4\mathbb{N}$, an edge-weighted complete graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with weight function $w: E(G) \to [0,1]$ is guaranteed to contain a $K_4$-factor where each copy of $K_4$ has total weight strictly greater than $6t$, provided that the minimum weighted degree satisfies $\delta^w(G) \geq (f(t) + \mu)n$. What is the expression for the function $f(t)$?

Given integers $r>d\ge 0$ and an $r$-partite graph, an independent $(r-d)$-transversal is an independent set of size $r-d$ that intersects each part in at most one vertex. Let $c$ be the infimum over all real numbers $\alpha$ such that for any integer $\Delta \ge 1$, every $6$-partite graph with maximum degree $\Delta$ and all parts of size $n > \alpha \Delta$ is guaranteed to contain an independent $5$-transversal. What is the exact value of $c$?

Consider the 3D Patlak-Keller-Segel-Navier-Stokes system in the periodic domain $\mathbb{T}^{3}$ with an imposed Couette flow $(Ay, 0, 0)$. Assume the standard normalization where the corresponding 2D Keller-Segel system has a critical mass of $8\pi$. If the Couette flow is sufficiently strong (i.e., $A$ is large enough) and the zero modes of the initial velocity are sufficiently small, there exists a sharp critical mass threshold $M_c$ such that the solutions to the system are global in time provided the initial cell mass is strictly less than $M_c$. What is the value of this sharp critical mass threshold $M_c$?

Consider the nonlinear integro-differential equation on the Heisenberg group $\mathbb{H}^n$ with homogeneous dimension $Q = 2n+2$:
$$ u_t - \Delta_{\mathbb{H}} u = \int_0^t (t-s)^{-\gamma} |u(s)|^{p-1}u(s) \, ds $$
for $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ and $t > 0$, where $\Delta_{\mathbb{H}}$ is the sub-Laplacian. Let $p_c$ be the critical exponent such that for all $1 < p \leq p_c$, no global positive solution exists for any non-trivial non-negative initial data, while for $p > p_c$, a global positive solution exists if the non-negative initial data is sufficiently small. What is the expression for $p_c$ in terms of $\gamma$ and $Q$?

Consider a branching Brownian motion in $\mathbb{R}^2$ in which particles independently diffuse as standard Brownian motions and branch at an inhomogeneous rate $b(\theta)$ depending only on the polar angle $\theta$ of the particle's position. Assume that the branching rate $b$ is a continuous function that reaches its maximum on $[-\pi, \pi]$ at $\theta=0$ only, with the asymptotic behavior $b(\theta) = 1 - \beta|\theta|^\alpha + O(\theta^2)$ as $\theta\to 0$, for constants $\alpha\in (2/3,2)$ and $\beta>0$. Let $M_t$ be the maximum distance to the origin among all particles alive at time $t$. It is known that there exists a centering function $m(t)$ such that the sequence $(M_t-m(t))_{t\ge 1}$ is tight, and $m(t)$ admits the asymptotic expansion
$$ m(t) = \sqrt{2} t - C t^\gamma - D \log t $$
where $C>0$ and $D$ are constants, with $C$ depending on the first eigenvalue of a specific associated operator. What is the exponent $\gamma$ as a function of $\alpha$?

Let $E^n_q$ be the set of all $n$-dimensional vectors over an alphabet of size $q$. For a subset $A \subseteq E^n_q$, its combinatorial rank $R(A)$ is defined as the number of non-constant columns in the $|A| \times n$ matrix whose rows are the elements of $A$. Let $D_A$ be the sum of the Hamming distances between all unordered pairs of vectors in $A$. Assuming $|A|/q \ge 1$, what is the tight lower bound for $R(A)$ in terms of $D_A$, $q$, and $|A|$?

Let $h$ be the spatial mesh size. Consider finite volume and Runge-Kutta discontinuous Galerkin methods with abstract limiting for hyperbolic systems of conservation laws in 1D. Assume that the exact solution is piecewise Lipschitz continuous, its finitely many and isolated shock curves can be traced with precision $h^{2/3}$, and outside of these shock tracing tubular neighborhoods the numerical solution (assumed to be uniformly small in BV) has oscillation strength $h$ across each mesh cell and cell boundary. Under a time step restriction $\tau \le c h$, a conditional a priori error estimate establishes convergence in the $L^\infty L^1$ norm with a rate of $h^\alpha$. What is the exact value of the exponent $\alpha$?

Let $\Sigma$ be a 2-letter alphabet and let $w$ be an infinite word over $\Sigma$. A finite factor (contiguous subword) $f$ of $w$ is called an \emph{imaged} factor if there exists a non-erasing morphism $m: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$, distinct from the identity morphism, such that $m(f)$ is also a factor of $w$. What is the maximum integer $N$ such that every infinite binary word $w$ is guaranteed to contain at least $N$ distinct imaged factors?

Let $\omega(n)$ denote the sum of the squares of the coefficients of the polynomial 
$$P_n(x) = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} \left(1 + x^{2^i+1} + x^{2^{i+1}+1}\right)$$
What is the exact number of decimal digits in the base-10 representation of $\omega(10000)$?

Let $\Delta$ be a positive integer. For a simple connected graph $G$ with maximum degree $\Delta$, let $\overleftrightarrow{G}$ be the symmetric digraph obtained by replacing each edge $uv$ of $G$ with a pair of opposite arcs $\overrightarrow{uv}$ and $\overrightarrow{vu}$. An arc-colouring $c$ of $\overleftrightarrow{G}$ is called *distinguishing* if the only automorphism of $\overleftrightarrow{G}$ preserving the colouring $c$ is the identity. A *proper arc-colouring of type I* is an arc-colouring such that any two consecutive arcs $\overrightarrow{uv}$ and $\overrightarrow{vw}$ have distinct colours. Let $k(G)$ denote the least number of colours required for a distinguishing proper arc-colouring of type I of $\overleftrightarrow{G}$. What is the supremum of $k(G)$ over all simple connected graphs $G$ with maximum degree $\Delta$, expressed as a function of $\Delta$?

Let $G$ be a compact simple Lie group with identity element $e$. A Rota-Baxter operator on $G$ is defined as a smooth map $B : G \to G$ satisfying the equation $B(g)B(h) = B(gB(g)hB(g)^{-1})$ for all $g, h \in G$. Find the set of all possible formulas for $B(g)$ representing the complete classification of Rota-Baxter operators on $G$. Express your answer as a set of expressions in terms of $g$ and $e$.

Let $Cay(S_n,T_n)$ be the Cayley graph on the symmetric group $S_n$ generated by the set $T_n$ of all transpositions. A conjecture by Kravchuk states that there exists a constant $n_0$ such that for $n \geq n_0$, each integer in the symmetric interval $[-f(n), f(n)]$ is an eigenvalue of $Cay(S_n,T_n)$. What is the exact expression for $f(n)$ as a function of $n$ according to this conjecture?

A transparent rectangle visibility graph (TRVG) is a graph whose vertices can be represented by a collection of non-overlapping rectangles in the plane whose sides are parallel to the axes such that two vertices are adjacent if and only if there is a horizontal or vertical line intersecting the interiors of their rectangles. Let $S$ be the set of all pairs of integers $(p,q)$ with $3 \le p \le q$ such that the complete bipartite graph $K_{p,q}$ is a TRVG. What is the set $S$?

Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ ($n \ge 2$) be a bounded $C^1$-domain. For every Radon measure $f$ on $\Omega$ with finite total variation, let $u \in W_0^{1,1}(\Omega)$ be the unique weak solution of the Poisson equation $-\Delta u = f$ in $\Omega$ satisfying $\nabla u \in L^{n/(n-1),\infty}(\Omega;\mathbb{R}^n)$. It is established that $u$ possesses optimal regularity in Sobolev-Lorentz spaces $L_s^{p,q}(\Omega)$, specifically $u \in L_{\alpha+1}^{p(\alpha),\infty}(\Omega)$ for any $0 \le \alpha < 1$. What is the exact exponent $p(\alpha)$ as a function of $n$ and $\alpha$?

Consider the standard Legendrian Hopf link in the standard contact 3-sphere. Up to compactly supported Hamiltonian isotopy, what is the exact number of oriented embedded exact Lagrangian fillings of this link in the standard symplectic Darboux ball?

Let $X$ be a smooth cubic threefold over the complex numbers. For degree $e \geqslant 5$, what is the exact number of irreducible main components of the Kontsevich moduli space $\overline{M}_{1,0}(X, e)$ of genus one stable maps to $X$?

Let $X$ be an $n$-dimensional K-semistable Fano manifold that is not isomorphic to the projective space $\mathbb{P}^n$. What is the maximum possible value of the anti-canonical volume of $X$, as an expression in terms of $n$?

Let $p_0(z) = 0$ and $p_{m+1}(z) = p_m(z)^2 + z$ for $m \ge 0$. Define the Misiurewicz-Thurston polynomial $q_{\ell,n}(z) = p_{\ell+n}(z) - p_\ell(z)$ for integers $\ell \ge 1$ and $n \ge 1$. Let $k$ be a divisor of $n$, and let $z_0 \in \mathbb{C}$ be a parameter such that the sequence $(p_m(z_0))_{m \ge 1}$ is purely periodic with exact period $k$. What is the algebraic multiplicity of $z_0$ as a root of $q_{\ell,n}(z)$? Express your answer in terms of $\ell$ and $k$.

Consider an integer sequence $a(k)$ defined by $a(1)=x$ (where $x$ is an integer), and for $k > 1$, $a(k) = a(k-1) + y$ if $k \in \{a(1), a(2), \ldots, a(k-1)\}$, and $a(k) = a(k-1) + z$ otherwise. Assuming $y > z > 0$, determine the limit $\lim_{k 	o \infty}  rac{a(k)}{k}$ as a function of $y$ and $z$.

Consider the canonical ensemble thermodynamics of a gauged permutation invariant tensor quantum harmonic oscillator. The system is formulated in terms of tensor variables $\Phi_{i_1, \dots, i_s}$, where each of the $s$ indices ranges over $\{1, 2, \dots, N\}$, and their conjugate momenta. The symmetric group $S_N$ acts on these variables via the standard $S_N$ action of permutations on the index values. The partition function is defined by projecting the thermal trace onto the $S_N$-invariant sector (i.e., gauging the $S_N$ symmetry), with zero-point energies shifted to zero such that the Boltzmann factor is $x = e^{-\beta}$. The high-temperature expansion of this partition function in terms of $x$ breaks down at a critical Boltzmann factor $x_c$. What is the leading approximation for $x_c$ in the large $N$ limit?

Let $\operatorname{LIS}(\sigma_n)$ be the length of the longest increasing subsequence of a permutation $\sigma_n$ of size $n$ sampled from the Brownian separable permuton $\boldsymbol{\mu}_p$ of parameter $p\in(0,1)$. As $n\to\infty$, it is established that $\frac{\operatorname{LIS}(\sigma_n)}{n^\alpha} \xrightarrow{a.s.} X$, where $X$ is a non-deterministic and almost surely positive and finite random variable. The exponent $\alpha \in (1/2, 1)$ is uniquely determined as the solution to the equation $f(\alpha) = \frac{p}{p-1}$. What is the explicit expression for the function $f(\alpha)$ in terms of $\alpha$, involving the Gamma function?

Let $\ell$ and $n$ be positive integers with $\ell$ prime. In the arithmetic of modular curves, an algebraic point $x$ on a curve defined over $\mathbb{Q}$ is called isolated if it does not belong to any infinite parameterized family of points having the same degree. Over all primes $\ell$ and positive integers $n$, exactly how many $j$-invariants in $\mathbb{Q}$ arise as the image of an isolated point $x \in X_1(\ell^n)$ under the natural map $j: X_1(\ell^n) \rightarrow X_1(1)$?

Given an origami crease pattern $C=(V,E)$ consisting of straight lines in $\mathbb{R}^2$, a mountain-valley (MV) assignment $\mu:E\to\{-1,1\}$ is locally valid if the faces around each vertex in $C$ can be folded flat under $\mu$. The origami flip graph $OFG(C)$ is a graph whose vertices are all locally valid MV assignments of $C$, and two vertices are adjacent if they differ by a face flip (an operation that swaps the MV-parity of every crease bordering a single face of $C$). Let $M_{2,n}$ denote the standard $2\times n$ Miura-ori crease pattern, which is a $2\times n$ parallelogram tessellation. What is the diameter of the graph $OFG(M_{2,n})$ as a function of $n$?

Let $C(G)$ denote the cycle space of a graph $G$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$, and let $C_n(G)$ denote the subspace of $C(G)$ spanned by the incidence vectors of the Hamilton cycles of $G$. For a random graph $G \sim \mathbb{G}(n,p)$ with $n$ being an odd integer, there is an integer $k$ such that the minimum degree condition $\delta(G) \geq k$ is asymptotically almost surely necessary and sufficient to ensure that $C_n(G) = C(G)$. What is the value of $k$?

Let $X \subset \mathbb{P}^n$ be a nonsingular hypersurface of degree $d$, with $n \geq 4$ and $d \geq 2$. What is the dimension of the space $H^1(X, \operatorname{End}(T_X))$ of infinitesimal deformations of the tangent bundle $T_X$?

The Pythagoras number $p(F)$ of a field $F$ is defined as the smallest positive integer $n$ such that every sum of squares in $F$ can be written as a sum of at most $n$ squares. What is the maximum value of $p(F)$ among all fields $F$ of transcendence degree $1$ over $\mathbb{Q}$?

Consider the gravitational field equations of scalar field cosmology for a quintessence scalar field $\phi$ in a spatially flat Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker (FLRW) background with an uncoupled dust fluid source. The scalar field potential is $V(\phi) = \alpha(e^{\lambda \phi} + \beta)$, where $\alpha, \beta,$ and $\lambda$ are constants. Painlevé analysis establishes that these cosmological field equations possess the Painlevé property and are integrable strictly when $\lambda^2 > c$. What is the exact numerical value of the constant $c$?

Consider semi-stable radial solutions $u \in H^1(B_1)$ of the general Hardy-Henon equation $-\Delta u = |x|^\alpha f(u)$ in $B_1 \setminus \{0\}$, where $f \in C^1(\mathbb{R})$, $\alpha > -2$, and $B_1$ is the unit ball of $\mathbb{R}^N$ for $N > 1$. There exists a critical dimension threshold $N_c(\alpha)$ such that for all dimensions $2 \leq N < N_c(\alpha)$, all such semi-stable radial solutions are bounded, while for $N \geq N_c(\alpha)$, there exist semi-stable radially decreasing unbounded $H^1(B_1)$ solutions. What is the expression for $N_c(\alpha)$ in terms of $\alpha$?
What is the maximum number of lines on a complex non-K3 quartic surface that is not ruled by lines?

Let $d$ be the minimum integer such that for every prime order group $G$ and every finite-dimensional representation of $G$ over the field of rational numbers $\mathbb{Q}$, the polynomial invariants of degree at most $d$ separate the orbits. What is the value of $d$?

Let $h(t,x)$ be the solution to the Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) equation with narrow wedge initial data, which can be formally defined via the Cole-Hopf transformation as $h(t,x) = \log Z(t,x)$, where $Z(t,x)$ solves the stochastic heat equation $\partial_t Z = \frac{1}{2}\partial_x^2 Z + \xi Z$ with Dirac delta initial condition $Z(0,x) = \delta_0(x)$ and $\xi$ being the standard space-time white noise. For a fixed $t>0$, what is the function $f(t,x)$ such that the spatial covariance satisfies ${\rm Cov}[h(t,x),h(t,0)] \sim f(t,x)$ as $x\to\infty$ (meaning their ratio converges to 1)?

Let $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{3,n}$ be the moduli space of stable curves of genus $3$ with $n$ marked points. What is the minimum integer $n$ such that $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{3,n}$ is of general type?

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected simple graph. For a subset $D \subseteq V$ with $|D| \geq 2$, a $D$-path is a path that includes all vertices in $D$. A collection of such paths is internally disjoint if they intersect only at the vertices of $D$ and share no edges. Let $\pi_G(D)$ denote the maximum number of internally disjoint $D$-paths in $G$. The $k$-path-connectivity is defined as $\pi_k(G) = \min \{ \pi_G(D) \mid D \subseteq V(G),\ |D| = k\}$. Let $AQ_n$ denote the $n$-dimensional augmented cube graph. What is the value of the 3-path-connectivity $\pi_3(AQ_{10})$?

The group of automorphisms of the free group on two generators, $\mathrm{Aut}(F_2)$, is known to act geometrically, in an essentially unique way, on a 2-dimensional CAT(0) space $X$. A Hamiltonian surface in $X$ is defined as a surface in $X$ which visits every vertex and every edge precisely once. Exactly how many Hamiltonian surfaces does $X$ contain?

Let $r$ denote the level of the Schmüdgen-type moment-SOS hierarchy. Consider the approximation of truncated moment sequences for compact domains that either satisfy the Polyak-Lojasiewicz condition or are defined by locally strongly convex polynomials. The convergence rate of the hierarchy, measured by the Hausdorff distance between the set of truncated pseudo-moment sequences and the set of truncated moment sequences specified by Tchakaloff's theorem, is established to be of the order $\mathcal{O}(r^{-\alpha})$. What is the exact value of the exponent $\alpha$?

Consider the Poisson equation with rough boundary data $g \in L^2(\partial \Omega)$ and source term $f \in L^2(\Omega)$ on a convex domain $\Omega$. When using the lowest-order Raviart--Thomas mixed finite element method ($\mathcal{R T}_0 \times \mathcal{D G}_0$) to solve this problem directly (without boundary data regularization), the numerical solution $u_h$ converges to the very weak solution $u$. The theoretically established convergence rate of the numerical solution $u_h$ in the $L^2(\Omega)$-norm with respect to the mesh size $h$ is $\mathcal{O}(h^\alpha)$. What is the exact value of the exponent $\alpha$?
Let $E$ be the set of all positive integers $n$ such that there does not exist a loop-free inseparable cubic graph (possibly non-simple) containing exactly $n$ cycles. What is the set $E$?

Let $G$ be a finite group. Let $\rho(G) = \prod_{g \in G} o(g)$ be the product of the orders of all elements in $G$. Let $\rho(G) = p_1^{\alpha_1} p_2^{\alpha_2} \cdots p_k^{\alpha_k}$ be the prime factorization of $\rho(G)$, where the $p_i$ are distinct primes, and define the set of exponents as $\operatorname{Exp}_\rho(G) = \{\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \cdots, \alpha_k\}$. It is known that there is a unique non-abelian finite group $G$ such that $\operatorname{Exp}_\rho(G) = \operatorname{Exp}_\rho(\mathbb{Z}_{2qr})$ for some distinct odd prime numbers $q$ and $r$. What is the order of this group $G$?

Let the metric dimension reduction modulus $k^\alpha_n(\ell_\infty)$ be the smallest integer $k$ such that every $n$-point metric space can be embedded into some $k$-dimensional normed space with bi-Lipschitz distortion at most $\alpha$. For any fixed constant $\beta > 0$ and for every $\alpha$ satisfying $\beta \log n \leq \alpha \leq n$, what is the sharp asymptotic order of $k^\alpha_n(\ell_\infty)$? Provide the answer in Big-$\Theta$ notation in terms of $n$ and $\alpha$.

Let $G$ be a simple graph with $|V| > 1$ vertices, and let $\mathfrak{n}_k(G)$ be the $k$-step nilpotent Lie algebra associated with $G$. Suppose $\mathfrak{n}_k(G)$ is rigid in the variety of at most $k$-step nilpotent Lie algebras, and $G$ is not a complete graph. It has been proven that such rigid examples exist for exactly one integer value of the step size $k \ge 2$, and only for graphs $G$ having at most $V_{\max}$ vertices. What is the ordered pair $(k, V_{\max})$?

Let $C(G)$ denote the cycle space of a graph $G$, defined as the vector space over $\mathbb{F}_2$ spanned by all incidence vectors of edge-sets of cycles of $G$. For an $n$-vertex graph $G$, let $C_k(G)$ denote the subspace of $C(G)$ spanned by the incidence vectors of cycles of length $k$ in $G$. Consider the random $d$-regular graph $G_{n,d}$ on $n$ vertices. When $n$ is odd and $d$ is a sufficiently large even integer, it is known that $C_n(G_{n,d}) = C(G_{n,d})$ asymptotically almost surely. When $n$ is an even integer and $d$ is a sufficiently large integer, there is a specific cycle length $k$ such that $C_k(G_{n,d}) = C(G_{n,d})$ asymptotically almost surely. What is this value of $k$ as a function of $n$?

Consider learning a target density $p^\star$ on $\mathbb{R}^d$ (where $d \ge 3$) that lies in a nonparametric $\beta$-smooth Hölder class (with $\beta>0$) and has either compact support or sub-Gaussian tails, using an i.i.d. sample of size $n$. For score-based generative models trained via denoising score matching using neural network-based score estimators, the convergence rate under the $1$-Wasserstein distance is shown to achieve the minimax rate, which scales as $n^\gamma$ up to polylogarithmic factors. What is the exponent $\gamma$ as a function of $\beta$ and $d$?

Let $\mathfrak{go}(n)$ denote the Lie algebra composed of all superoperators that conserve the Gaussianity of an $n$-mode continuous-variable bosonic quantum state. It is known that $\mathfrak{go}(n)$ is isomorphic to a semidirect sum of the form $\mathbb{R}^{d} \oplus_{\mathrm{S}} \mathfrak{gl}(2n, \mathbb{R})$. What is the dimension $d$ of the real vector space component as a function of the number of modes $n$?

Let $q>1$ and let $\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a Lipschitz domain. Any planar mapping $f\in W^{2,q}(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^2)$ that agrees with a homeomorphism on the boundary $\partial \Omega$ and satisfies $|J_f|^{-a}\in L^1(\Omega)$ is guaranteed to be a homeomorphism on $\Omega$, provided that $a$ is greater than or equal to a sharp lower bound depending on $q$. What is this sharp lower bound on $a$ as a function of $q$?

Deeley constructed a counterexample to the HK-conjecture (which relates to the stable and unstable groupoids of a Smale space) using an odometer built from a flat manifold of dimension 9 and an expansive self-cover. What is the minimal dimension $d$ of a flat manifold for which there exists an odometer, built from an expansive self-cover of the manifold, that provides a counterexample to the HK-conjecture?

What is the smallest odd prime power $q$ such that every square matrix over the finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$ can be decomposed as a sum $D+M$, where $D$ is a diagonalizable matrix over $\mathbb{F}_q$ and $M$ is a matrix over $\mathbb{F}_q$ satisfying $M^2=0$?

A subset of vertices $S$ of a graph $G$ is called a distinguishing set if the only automorphism $g \in \text{Aut}(G)$ that preserves $S$ as a set (i.e., $g(S) = S$) is the identity automorphism. The distinguishing cost of $G$ is the minimal cardinality of a distinguishing set. What is the distinguishing cost of any connected cubic vertex-transitive graph of girth 5 that has exactly two edge orbits?

Let $G$ be an edge-weighted graph with non-negative edge weights, total edge weight $W$, and maximum weighted degree $\Delta$. For any integer $k \geq 2$, there always exists a $k$-partition $(V_1, V_2, \dots , V_k)$ of the vertex set $V(G)$ such that the maximum total edge weight of an induced subgraph, $\max_{i=1}^k w(G[V_i])$, is bounded above by a tight bound of the form $A(k) W + B(k) \Delta$, where $A(k)$ and $B(k)$ are functions of $k$ only. What is this exact upper bound expression? Provide the answer in terms of $k$, $W$, and $\Delta$.

Let $X$ be a centered real-valued sub-Gaussian random variable. Define the sub-Gaussian norm as $\|X\|_{\psi_2} = \inf \{ t > 0 : \mathbb{E}[\exp(X^2/t^2)] \le 2 \}$, and the sub-Gaussian parameter $\sigma_X$ as the infimum over all $\sigma \ge 0$ such that $\mathbb{E}[\exp(\lambda X)] \le \exp(\sigma^2 \lambda^2 / 2)$ for all $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$. What is the optimal (smallest) constant $C > 0$ such that $\sigma_X \le C \|X\|_{\psi_2}$ for all such random variables $X$?

Let $H$ be a spherical Hopf algebra, and let $M$ be a $3$-dimensional closed connected oriented manifold. Let $\mathcal{K}$ denote the chromatic spherical invariant of $M$ associated to the pivotal category of finite-dimensional $H$-modules. The invariant $\mathcal{K}$ is equal to the Hennings-Kauffman-Radford invariant $\operatorname{HKR}$ of $M$ associated to a specific Hopf algebra constructed from $H$. In terms of $H$, what is this Hopf algebra?

Let $J$ denote the Jacobian of the odd hyperelliptic curve $C : y^2 = f(x)$ of genus $g \geq 1$. A "density 1" form of the Lang--Silverman conjecture establishes that $100\%$ of monic polynomials $f(x) \in \mathbb{Z}[x]$ of degree $2g+1$ and nonzero discriminant $\Delta(f)$ have the property that, for any non-trivial point $P \in J(\mathbb{Q})$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, the canonical height of $P$ satisfies the lower bound:
$$ \widehat{h}_\Theta(P) \geq \left( c(g) - \epsilon \right) \log |\Delta(f)| $$
What is the specific rational function $c(g)$ that appears in this established bound?

Let $X$ be a smooth projective hypersurface of dimension at least three that is not a quadric threefold. What is the number of irreducible components of logarithmic type in the space of codimension-one foliations of degree one on $X$?

Consider the multi-dimensional Hegselmann-Krause (HK) dynamics driven by symmetric i.i.d. noise with zero mean, non-zero variance, and bounded norm $\|\xi_i(t)\| \leq \delta \leq \epsilon/2$ in an unbounded continuous space $\mathbb{R}^d$ with confidence threshold $\epsilon$. What is the maximum integer dimension $d$ for which quasi-synchronization is achieved almost surely in finite time for all initial states?

Consider linear kinetic equations (such as the kinetic Fokker-Planck equation or linear relaxation equation) for a distribution function in a spatial half-space $\mathbb{R}^d_+$ with absorbing boundary conditions. For large times $t$, the solutions decay in a suitable weighted $L^\infty$ space at a rate proportional to $t^{-\alpha}$, matching the large-time decay of solutions to the heat equation in the half-space with Dirichlet boundary conditions. What is the exponent $\alpha$ as a function of the spatial dimension $d$?

Consider the diagonal embedding of $\mathrm{GL}_{2}(q)$ into the product group $\mathrm{GL}_{2}(q)\times\mathrm{GL}_{2}(q)$ over a finite field of order $q$. What is the set of dimensions of the irreducible representations of $\mathrm{GL}_{2}(q)$ for which the induced representation to $\mathrm{GL}_{2}(q)\times\mathrm{GL}_{2}(q)$ decomposes multiplicity free?

Let $ex_{\mathcal{P}}(n, H)$ denote the maximum number of edges in a simple planar graph on $n$ vertices that does not contain $H$ as a subgraph. A $(h,k)$-quasi-double star $W_{h,k}$ is the graph obtained from a path on 3 vertices $v_1v_2v_3$ by adding $h$ leaves to $v_1$ and $k$ leaves to $v_3$. For integers $h$ and $k$ such that $1 \le h \le 2 \le k \le 5$ and $3 \le h+k \le 5$, what is the exact value of $ex_{\mathcal{P}}(n,W_{h,k})$ for $n$ such that $h+k+2$ divides $n$?

Let $M = (E, \mathcal{I})$ be an arbitrary matroid whose ground set $E$ can be partitioned into $k \ge 2$ bases. Let $S_1$ and $S_2$ be two arbitrary disjoint subsets of $E$. It is a proven theorem that there always exists a partition of $E$ into $k$ bases $B_1, \dots, B_k$ such that $\max_{1 \le i, j \le k} \left| |B_i \cap S_1| - |B_j \cap S_1| \right| \le 1$. Let $D$ be the minimum integer such that, for every such matroid and every choice of disjoint subsets $S_1, S_2 \subseteq E$, there exists a partition of $E$ into $k$ bases $B_1, \ldots, B_k$ that simultaneously satisfies $\max_{1 \le i, j \le k} \left| |B_i \cap S_1| - |B_j \cap S_1| \right| \le 1$ and $\max_{1 \le i, j \le k} \left| |B_i \cap S_2| - |B_j \cap S_2| \right| \le D$. What is the value of $D$?

Let $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$. An $\alpha$-stationary surface in Euclidean space is a surface $\Sigma$ whose mean curvature $H$ satisfies $H(p) = \alpha|p|^{-2} \langle \nu, p\rangle$ for all $p \in \Sigma$, where $\nu$ is the unit normal vector. It is known that a spatial inversion with respect to the origin, $p \mapsto \frac{p}{|p|^2}$, maps any $\alpha$-stationary surface to a $\beta$-stationary surface. What is $\beta$ as a function of $\alpha$?
What is the maximum possible number of edges in an $n$-vertex $2$-connected graph that contains no cycle of length $m$ such that $m \equiv 0 \pmod 4$, assuming $n \geq 12$?

Let $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$ and let $\vec{v} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ be a fixed unit vector. A singular minimal surface $\Sigma$ in Euclidean space is defined as a surface whose mean curvature $H$ satisfies the equation $H = \alpha \frac{\langle N, \vec{v} \rangle}{\langle p, \vec{v} \rangle}$, where $N$ is the unit normal vector of $\Sigma$ and $p$ is the position vector on $\Sigma$. Suppose that $\Sigma$ is a singular minimal surface (with $\alpha \neq 0$) which is invariant under a continuous one-parameter group of helicoidal motions with non-zero pitch. What is the exact value of the constant $\alpha$?

Let $G$ be a graph with order $n$ and clique number $\omega$. Let $\dim_l(G)$ denote the local metric dimension of $G$. A known conjecture, which has been verified for $K_5$-free graphs, states that if $n \geq \omega + 1 \geq 4$, then $\dim_l(G)$ is bounded above by a specific function of the form $f(\omega)n$. What is the exact expression for this upper bound $f(\omega)n$ in terms of $\omega$ and $n$?

Let $U$ be a connected closed oriented surface of positive genus minus a finite nonempty set, and let $U^*$ be its one-point compactification with the added point $*$. For an integer $n \ge 1$, let $\text{Conf}_n(U)$ denote the configuration space of $n$ ordered distinct points in $U$. The mapping class group $\text{Mod}(U)$ of $U$ acts naturally on both the fundamental group $\pi_1(U^*, *)$ and the cohomology group $H^k(\text{Conf}_n(U))$. It is a theorem that the action of $\text{Mod}(U)$ on $H^k(\text{Conf}_n(U))$ factors through its action on the nilpotent quotient $\pi_1(U^*, *) / \gamma_m(\pi_1(U^*, *))$, where $\gamma_m(G)$ denotes the $m$-th term of the lower central series of a group $G$ (defined such that $\gamma_1(G) = G$). What is the exact value of the index $m$ as a function of $k$?

Let $\operatorname{OD}_{n}$ be the monoid of all order-preserving functions and order-reversing functions on the set $\{1,\ldots,n\}$. Let $\Bbbk$ be a field whose characteristic is not $2$. The quiver of the monoid algebra $\Bbbk\operatorname{OD}_{n}$ consists of exactly two connected components, each of which is a straightline path. What is the unordered pair representing the number of vertices in each of these two paths?

Let $\alpha \in (0,1)$ and let $b \ge 2$ be an integer. The $\alpha$-Weierstrass function is defined as $W_g^{\alpha,b}(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} b^{-\alpha k} g(b^k x)$, where $g$ is a Lipschitz function on the unit circle. What is the Hausdorff dimension of almost every level set of a prevalent $\alpha$-Weierstrass function (with respect to its occupation measure)?

Let $ex_{\mathcal{P}}(n,H)$ denote the planar Turán number of a graph $H$, defined as the maximum number of edges in an $n$-vertex planar graph that does not contain $H$ as a subgraph. Let $C_3 \cup C_5$ denote the vertex-disjoint union of a cycle of length 3 and a cycle of length 5. What is the exact value of $ex_{\mathcal{P}}(n, C_3 \cup C_5)$ for sufficiently large $n$?

The representation number of a graph is the minimum number of copies of each vertex required to represent the graph as a word, such that the letters corresponding to vertices $x$ and $y$ alternate if and only if $xy$ is an edge in the graph. For integers $m \ge 3$ and $n \ge 3$, what is the representation number of the $m \times n$ grid graph?

The Maker-Breaker domination game is played on a finite simple graph $G$ by Dominator and Staller who alternate turns selecting an unplayed vertex of $G$. The goal of Dominator is that the vertices he selected during the game form a dominating set, while Staller's goal is to prevent this from happening. The graph invariant $\gamma_{\rm MB}'(G)$ is the number of Dominator's moves in the game played on $G$ in which he can achieve his goal when Staller makes the first move and both players play optimally. A graph $G$ is defined as $2$-$\gamma_{\rm MB}'$-critical if $\gamma_{\rm MB}'(G)=2$ and $\gamma_{\rm MB}'(G-e)>2$ for every edge $e$ in $G$. Up to isomorphism, what is the unique connected, non-bipartite, triangle-free $2$-$\gamma_{\rm MB}'$-critical graph?

Let $n$ and $t$ be integers such that $2 \leq t \leq c n$ for a sufficiently small absolute constant $c>0$. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a family of spanning trees of the complete graph $K_n$ such that the edge sets of any two trees in $\mathcal{A}$ intersect in at least $t$ edges. What is the maximum possible cardinality of $\mathcal{A}$ as a function of $n$ and $t$?

Let $L$ be a non-split adequate link with Turaev genus one. Let $c(L)$ denote the crossing number of $L$. What is the span of the Jones polynomial of $L$ (defined as the difference between the maximum and minimum degrees of the polynomial)?

Consider the one-dimensional tight-binding model with $n$-th-nearest-neighbor hopping. When formulating an exact cyclic reduction-based real-space renormalization scheme using quantics tensor trains (QTT), what is the exact QTT bond dimension of the one-particle Green's function?

Given a graph $G$ and a subgraph $H$ of $G$, the odd Ramsey number $r_{odd}(G,H)$ is defined as the minimum number of colors needed to edge-color $G$ so that every copy of $H$ intersects some color class in an odd number of edges. Let $K_{n,n}$ denote the complete bipartite graph with two parts of size $n$, and $K_{2,t}$ denote the complete bipartite graph with parts of size $2$ and $t$. For a fixed integer $t \geq 2$, the asymptotic behavior of the odd Ramsey number is given by $r_{odd}(K_{n,n}, K_{2,t}) = c_t n + o(n)$ as $n \to \infty$. What is the exact expression for the coefficient $c_t$ as a function of $t$?

A negatively curved hyperbolic cone metric on a surface is defined to be rigid if it is uniquely determined by the support of its Liouville current, and flexible otherwise. What is the exact number of mapping class group orbits of equivalence classes of flexible negatively curved hyperbolic cone metrics on the closed orientable surface of genus 2?

Consider the flux-limited chemotaxis system given by
$$ egin{cases} u_t = \Delta u - 
abla\cdot(u|
abla v|^{\alpha-2}
abla v), \ 0 = \Delta v + u, \end{cases}$$
posed in the unit ball of $\mathbb{R}^N$ for $N\geq2$, subject to no-flux boundary conditions for $u$ and homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions for $v$. For the critical flux limitation exponent $\alpha =  rac{N}{N-1}$ and any nonnegative, continuous, and radially symmetric initial datum $u_0$, the system admits a globally bounded weak solution if and only if the initial mass $\int u_0 dx$ is strictly less than a critical mass threshold $M_c$. What is the exact value of $M_c$? Express your answer in terms of $N$ and $\omega_N$, where $\omega_N$ denotes the $(N-1)$-dimensional surface measure of the unit sphere $\mathbb{S}^{N-1}$.

For the discrete Schrödinger equation on the Layered King's Grid, the sharp $l^1 \to l^\infty$ dispersive decay rate is proportional to $\langle t \rangle^\alpha$. What is the value of the exponent $\alpha$?

Let $X$ be a smooth projective complex variety of dimension $d$. There exists a birational morphism $X\times X\to Y$ to a projective variety $Y$ contracting the diagonal $\Delta_X\subset X\times X$ to a point if and only if $X$ has maximal Albanese dimension and its irregularity $q$ satisfies a lower bound $q \geq f(d)$. Provide the precise mathematical expression for $f(d)$.

Let $M$ be a closed, oriented smooth manifold and let $\operatorname{Prob}(M)$ denote the infinite-dimensional space of strictly positive probability densities on $M$. The Amari-Cencov $\alpha$-connections, denoted by $
abla^{(\alpha)}$, form a standard one-parameter family of affine connections on this space in information geometry. For exactly which set of values $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$ is the connection $
abla^{(\alpha)}$ on $\operatorname{Prob}(M)$ metric (i.e., there exists a Riemannian metric on $\operatorname{Prob}(M)$ whose Levi-Civita connection is exactly $
abla^{(\alpha)}$)?

Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a finite alphabet. Consider the space of continuous functions $F:\mathcal{A}^\mathbb{N}\to\mathcal{A}^\mathbb{N}$ equipped with the uniform topology. For any $F$ and $\varepsilon\in[0,1]$, let $F_\varepsilon$ denote the random dynamical system obtained by applying $F$ and then modifying each coordinate of the output independently with probability $\varepsilon$, replacing it with a value chosen uniformly at random from $\mathcal{A}$. Let $U(F)$ be the set of $\varepsilon\in[0,1]$ such that $F_\varepsilon$ has a unique invariant probability measure. For a generic function $F$ in the Baire category sense, what is the set $U(F)$?

Let $G_3$ be the standard Rubik's cube group, defined as the group of permutations of the movable facets of a $3 \times 3 \times 3$ Rubik's cube generated by the 90-degree rotations of its six faces. What is the minimal dimension of a faithful representation of $G_3$ over the complex numbers $\mathbb{C}$?

Let $R$ be an associative ring with identity. A ring $R$ is called strongly NUS-nil clean if for every non-unit $a \in R$, $a$ can be written as $a = n + e$ where $n$ is nilpotent, $e$ is a square-idempotent (i.e., $e^2$ is an idempotent, meaning $e^4=e^2$), and $ne = en$. There exists a unique monic polynomial $P(x) \in \mathbb{Z}[x]$ of minimal degree such that any ring $R$ is strongly NUS-nil clean if and only if $P(a)$ is nilpotent for every non-unit $a \in R$. What is the polynomial $P(x)$?

Let the $\tau$-independent function $\mathcal{R}^{K}_{n}(\chi)$ be defined as
$$ \mathcal{R}^{K}_{n}(\chi) = (2\pi)^{-3/2}\tanh^{-K}\chi\sinh^{-1/2}\chi[\Gamma(1+K)]^{-1}\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\omega\left (1-\frac{\cos \omega}{\cosh\chi}\right)^{K}e^{in\omega} $$
for any general $K$. The associated Legendre conical function of the first kind, $P^{-1/2-K}_{-1/2+i \tau}(\chi)$, can be expressed as a series that isolates the $\tau$ dependence into a specific function $f_n(\tau, \chi)$:
$$ P^{-1/2-K}_{-1/2+i \tau}(\chi) = 2\sum_{n}\mathcal{R}^{K}_{n}(\chi) f_n(\tau, \chi) $$
where $n$ ranges from $-\ell$ to $\ell$ in unit steps when $K$ is a non-negative integer $\ell$, and from $-\infty$ to $\infty$ in unit steps otherwise. What is the function $f_n(\tau, \chi)$?

Consider the strong well-posedness of McKean-Vlasov stochastic differential equations driven by an $\alpha$-stable process for $\alpha \in (0,2)$ with a Hölder (Besov) kernel $K \in \mathbf{C}^\beta$. What is the critical threshold function $f(\alpha)$ such that strong well-posedness is guaranteed for $\beta > f(\alpha)$, given that this condition coincides exactly with the well-known threshold for weak well-posedness?

Consider the problem of optimizing an $n$-variable quadratic function where each decision variable is constrained to be a complex $m$-th root of unity. At what level of the sum-of-squares (SOS) hierarchy is the hierarchy guaranteed to converge to the exact solution? Express your answer as a function of $n$ using the floor function.

Let $\{u_i(x)\}_{i=0}^d$ be the family of dual Hahn polynomials involving two real parameters $r$ and $s$. Let $L,L^*$ denote the corresponding Leonard pair. Assume that $d \geq 3$, $r \neq 0$ and $r+s=0$. It is known that $L, (L^*+c)^2$ is a Leonard pair, which establishes that the polynomials $\{u_i(x)\}_{i=0}^d$ are not only dual Hahn polynomials but also Racah polynomials with respect to the same inner product. What is the unique expression for the constant $c$ in terms of $r$ and $d$?

In the Temporal Graph Exploration problem, the input is a sequence of graphs $(G_i)_{i\in \mathbb{N}}$ on the same $n$-vertex set, and the goal is to find a walk of shortest length visiting all vertices, where the $i$-th step of the walk must traverse an edge present in $G_i$. Consider a random temporal graph generated by drawing each $G_i$ independently from an arbitrary probability distribution $\mu$ over the set of all $n$-vertex trees. It has been established that for any such distribution $\mu$, the temporal graph can be explored in $O(n^c)$ steps with high probability, and that there exist distributions $\mu$ for which $\Omega(n^c)$ steps are required with high probability. What is the exact value of the constant $c$?

For $n \in \{3, 4, 5\}$, there is an optimal decay constant $C_n$ such that if an orientable $n$-manifold $M$ admits a complete Riemannian metric whose scalar curvature is positive and has at most $C$-quadratic decay at infinity for some $C > C_n$, then strong topological restrictions are imposed (for $n=3$, $M$ necessarily decomposes as a possibly infinite connected sum of spherical manifolds and $\mathbb{S}^2\times \mathbb{S}^1$ summands; for $n \in \{4, 5\}$, topological obstructions exist to the existence of such metrics on certain noncompact contractible manifolds). What is the expression for this sharp decay constant $C_n$ as a function of $n$?

Let $G$ be a finite group. Let $\mathcal{O}^{\otimes}$ and $\mathcal{P}^{\otimes}$ be $k$- and $\ell$-connected almost-unital $G$-operads subject to the condition that for all $S$, $\mathcal{O}(S) = \emptyset$ if and only if $\mathcal{P}(S) = \emptyset$. The Boardman-Vogt tensor product $\mathcal{O}^{\otimes} \otimes \mathcal{P}^{\otimes}$ is guaranteed to be $m$-connected. What is the maximal guaranteed value of $m$?

Let $n$ be a positive integer that is a multiple of $3$. Let $\mathcal{M}$ be the set of all $n \times n$ matrices $A$ with non-negative real entries such that every entry satisfies $A_{ij} \le 1$. The spread of a matrix $A$ is defined as $\max_{i,j} |\lambda_i - \lambda_j|$, where $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n$ are the complex eigenvalues of $A$. What is the exact maximum spread of a matrix in $\mathcal{M}$, as a function of $n$?

Let $a_d(n)$ be the counting function for partitions of $n$ in which the odd parts are unrestricted and the even parts are $d$-colored. For each positive integer $\alpha$, there is a unique integer $\gamma_\alpha$ such that $0 \le \gamma_\alpha < 5^{2\alpha}$ and $a_3(5^{2\alpha}n + \gamma_\alpha) \equiv 0 \pmod{5^\alpha}$ holds for all integers $n \ge 0$. What is $\gamma_\alpha$ as a function of $\alpha$?

What is the minimum value of the difference $s'-r'$ for which there exists a non-constant holomorphic map from the Shilov boundary of the rank $1$ type-I bounded symmetric domain $\Omega_{1,s}$ (the unit sphere in $\mathbb{C}^s$) to the Shilov boundary of a type-I bounded symmetric domain $\Omega_{r',s'}$ of rank $r'$, where $s \ge 2$ and $2 \le r' \le s'$?

Let $G$ be an $\ell$-holed graph, meaning that all of its induced cycles of length at least four have length exactly $\ell$. Suppose that $\ell \geq 7$ is an odd integer. What is the sharp upper bound on the chromatic number $\chi(G)$ as a function of $\ell$ and the clique number $\omega(G)$?

Consider the semi-linear damped wave equation with power nonlinearity $|u|^p$ in $n$ spatial dimensions, where the initial data belongs to the homogeneous Sobolev space of negative order $\dot{H}_m^{-\gamma}$. What is the critical exponent $p_{\rm c}(m,\gamma,n)$ for $m \in (1, 2]$ and $\gamma \in [0, \frac{n(m-1)}{m})$ that separates the regime of global existence for small data ($p \geq p_{\rm c}$) from finite-time blow-up for small data ($1 < p < p_{\rm c}$)?

Consider one-dimensional, one-sided, nearest-neighbor Interacting Particle Systems (IPS) with positive rates. An ergodicity criterion establishes that such an IPS is ergodic if it admits a state for which the ratio of the rate of exiting the state to the rate of entering the state is strictly less than a specific constant $c$. What is the exact value of this constant $c$?

For systems undergoing phase-ordering kinetics after a quench into the ordered phase ($0<T<T_c$) from a fully disordered initial state with a non-conserved order-parameter, what is the low-temperature generalization of the Janssen-Schaub-Schmittmann scaling relation for the autocorrelation exponent $\lambda$ in terms of the spatial dimension $d$ and the exponent $\Theta$?

Let $S = K[x_1, \dots, x_n]$ be the standard graded polynomial ring over an arbitrary field $K$, with $n \ge 6$. What is the set of all integers $d \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ such that for every squarefree monomial ideal $I \subset S$ generated in degree $d$, $I$ has a linear resolution if and only if $I$ has linear powers?

The Pólya group $\mathrm{Po}(K)$ of a number field $K$ is the subgroup of the ideal class group $\mathrm{Cl}(K)$ of $K$ generated by the classes of all the products of the prime ideals of $K$ with the same norm. The Pólya index of $K$ is defined as the index $[\mathrm{Cl}(K):\mathrm{Po}(K)]$. Assuming the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis (GRH), exactly how many imaginary quadratic fields have a Pólya index of $2$?

Let $G$ be a five-dimensional, simply connected, nilpotent Lie group equipped with a left-invariant Riemannian metric, and let $\mathfrak{g}$ be its Lie algebra, which is canonically identified with the space of left-invariant vector fields on $G$. What subspace of $\mathfrak{g}$ coincides exactly with the space of all left-invariant Killing vector fields on $G$?

Consider the $D^{1,p}$-critical quasi-linear Hénon equation $-\Delta_p u = |x|^\alpha u^{p_\alpha^* - 1}$ in $\mathbb{R}^N$ with $u > 0$, where $N \geq 2$, $1 < p < N$, $\alpha > 0$, and $p_\alpha^* = \frac{p(N+\alpha)}{N-p}$. Using bifurcation theory, non-radial solutions with asymptotic behavior $u \sim |x|^{-\frac{N-p}{p-1}}$ and $|\nabla u| \sim |x|^{-\frac{N-1}{p-1}}$ at $\infty$ are proven to bifurcate from radial solutions when the parameter $\alpha$ takes certain critical values $\alpha(k)$ for integers $k \geq 2$ (where $k$ corresponds to the degree of spherical harmonics, and $\alpha(k) = 2(k-1)$ for the special case $p=2$). What is the exact algebraic expression for the critical values $\alpha(k)$ for general $1 < p < N$ as a function of $N$, $p$, and $k$?

Let $G = \mathrm{GL}_p(\mathbb{C})$ and let $V$ be the vector space of $n$-tuples of $p \times p$ matrices over $\mathbb{C}$, where $n \geq 2$ and $p \geq 2$. Consider the action of $G$ on $V$ by simultaneous conjugation. The separating variety $\mathcal{S}_{G,V}$ is the subvariety of $V^2$ consisting of pairs of points indistinguishable by invariant polynomials in $\mathbb{C}[V]^G$. What is the dimension of the separating variety $\mathcal{S}_{G,V}$ as a function of $n$ and $p$?

Let $G$ and $H$ be the non-isomorphic finite $2$-groups recently discovered as counterexamples to the Modular Isomorphism Problem, which have isomorphic group algebras over $\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$ but non-isomorphic group algebras over the $2$-adic integers $\mathbb{Z}_2$. What is the smallest positive integer $k$ such that the group algebras of $G$ and $H$ over the ring $\mathbb{Z}/2^k\mathbb{Z}$ are non-isomorphic?

Let $k'(G)$ be the number of conjugacy classes of subgroups of a finite group $G$, and let $L(G)$ be the set of all subgroups of $G$. Define the ratio $d'(G)=\frac{k'(G)}{|L(G)|}$. What is the topological closure in the standard topology of the real line of the set $\{ d'(G) \mid G \text{ is a finite nilpotent group} \}$?

Let $S_G^n$ denote the $n$-dimensional generalized Sierpiński graph with base graph $G$, and let $\chi_i'(H)$ denote the injective edge chromatic number of a graph $H$. What is the exact value of $\chi_i'(S_{K_3}^n)$ for all $n \ge 3$, where $K_3$ is the complete graph on 3 vertices?

Let $E$ be a bounded idempotent operator on a complex Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$, and let $I$ denote the identity operator on $\mathcal{H}$. Let $\mathcal{P}$ be the set of all orthogonal projections on $\mathcal{H}$, and let $m(E) \in \mathcal{P}$ be the matched projection of $E$, defined as the unique orthogonal projection that minimizes the operator norm distance to $E$. Consider the polar decomposition of the operator $2E-I$, written as $2E-I = U |2E-I|$, where $U$ is the unitary part. Express the unitary operator $U$ algebraically in terms of $m(E)$ and $I$.

Let $\operatorname{GL}(4, \mathbb{F}_2)$ be the set of all invertible $4 \times 4$ matrices over the finite field $\mathbb{F}_2$. What is the exact number of matrices in $\operatorname{GL}(4, \mathbb{F}_2)$ that cannot be expressed as the Hadamard (entrywise) product $A \circ B$ of two $4 \times 4$ matrices $A$ and $B$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$, where $\operatorname{rank}(A) = 2$ and $\operatorname{rank}(B) = 2$?

A $\delta$-derivation of a Lie algebra $L$ is a linear map $D: L \to L$ such that $D([x,y]) = \delta([D(x),y] + [x,D(y)])$ for all $x,y \in L$. It has been proven that every quasi-derivation of the Witt algebra can be expressed as a sum of a standard derivation (where $\delta=1$) and a $\delta$-derivation for a specific constant $\delta \neq 1$. What is the value of $\delta$?

A word-representable graph is a graph that can be represented by a word over its vertex set such that two vertices are adjacent if and only if their corresponding letters alternate in the word. Consider the class of graphs whose vertices can be partitioned into two cliques, such that one of the cliques has a size of exactly four and is maximal (i.e., no vertex in the other clique is connected to all vertices of this four-vertex clique). Up to isomorphism, what is the exact number of minimal non-word-representable graphs in this class?

Let $G$ be a simple undirected graph with $m$ edges and spectral radius $\lambda(G)$. Suppose that $\lambda(G) > \sqrt{m}$. It is a known theorem that there exist two vertices in $G$ having at least $c \sqrt{m} - O(1)$ common neighbors as $m \to \infty$. What is the supremum of the real constants $c$ for which this statement holds true for all such graphs?

An abstract rotation system of a complete graph $K_n$ assigns to each vertex a cyclic order of its incident edges. A rotation system is defined to be *generalized twisted* if it can be realized as a generalized twisted simple drawing (where any two edges intersect in at most one point, subject to specific geometric constraints). There is a minimal integer $k$ such that for any $n \geq 7$, an abstract rotation system of $K_n$ is generalized twisted if and only if all of its subrotation systems induced by $k$ vertices are generalized twisted. What is the value of $k$?

For integers $n, k, r$ with $k \geq 2$, $3 \leq r \leq 2k$, and $n \geq 2(r+2)(r+1)(r+2k)$, let $E(n,r)$ be the minimum integer such that any $n$-vertex $C_{2k+1}$-free graph $G$ with at least $E(n,r)$ edges is guaranteed to contain no odd cycles of length strictly greater than $r$. What is the exact formula for $E(n,r)$?
What is the maximum possible value of the $\mathbb{Q}$-Fano index of a canonical weak Fano $3$-fold?

Let $K$ be an algebraic number field with an elementary bicyclic 3-class group $\mathrm{Cl}_3(K) \simeq \mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$. Let $M = \mathrm{Gal}(F_3^{(2)}(K)/K)$ be the second 3-class group of $K$, where $F_3^{(2)}(K)$ is the second unramified 3-class field of $K$. Assume that $M$ is non-abelian. Let $E_1, E_2, E_3, E_4$ be the four unramified cyclic cubic extensions of $K$. Assume these extensions are ordered such that the orders of their 3-class groups satisfy $\lvert \mathrm{Cl}_3(E_1) \rvert \ge \lvert \mathrm{Cl}_3(E_2) \rvert \ge \lvert \mathrm{Cl}_3(E_3) \rvert \ge \lvert \mathrm{Cl}_3(E_4) \rvert$. Express the second largest order $\lvert \mathrm{Cl}_3(E_2) \rvert$ in terms of the coclass $c = \mathrm{cc}(M)$ of the group $M$.

Let $(A,\mathfrak m)$ be a two-dimensional excellent normal Gorenstein local domain containing an algebraically closed field. Let $I =H^0(X,\mathcal{O}_X(-Z)) \subset A$ be an $\mathfrak m$-primary integrally closed ideal represented by an anti-nef cycle $Z$ on some resolution $X\to \mathrm{Spec} A$. Let $r \ge 2$ denote the normal reduction number of $I$, and let $K_X$ denote the canonical divisor on $X$. Suppose the normal tangent cone $\overline{G}(I)$ is Cohen-Macaulay. It is proven that $\overline{G}(I)$ is Gorenstein if and only if $K_X Z$ is equal to a specific expression in terms of $r$ and $Z^2$. What is this expression?

A covering system of the integers is a finite collection of congruences $x \equiv a_i \pmod{m_i}$ for $1 \le i \le k$ such that every integer satisfies at least one congruence. What is the minimum possible value for the largest modulus $\max_{1 \le i \le k} m_i$ in a covering system where all moduli $m_i$ are distinct and the smallest modulus is exactly $\min_{1 \le i \le k} m_i = 5$?

Let $R_\alpha(x) = x + \alpha \pmod 1$ be an irrational rotation by angle $\alpha$ on the one-dimensional torus $\mathbb{T} = \mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$, and consider the observable $\phi(x) = \frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{1-x}$. Let $S_N(\phi)(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \phi(R_\alpha^k x)$ denote the corresponding Birkhoff sum. Let $A$ be the set of irrational numbers $\alpha \in \mathbb{T}$ such that, for Lebesgue almost every $x \in \mathbb{T}$, 
\[
\limsup_{N \to \infty} \frac{\phi(R_\alpha^N x)}{S_N(\phi)(x)} = 0.
\]
What is the Hausdorff dimension of the set $A$?

Let $d \ge 1$ be an integer. Consider the grid graphs arising from free Borel actions of $\mathbb{Z}^d$ on a standard Borel space. A $k$-polychromatic coloring is a coloring of the vertices such that every unit $d$-dimensional cube sees all $k$ available colors. What is the maximum integer $k$, as a function of $d$, for which it is guaranteed that every free Borel $\mathbb{Z}^d$-action on a standard Borel space admits a Borel $k$-polychromatic coloring?

Let $X \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ ($d > 7$) be a tight spherical $5$-design of minimal type. It is known that half of the derived code of $X$ forms an equiangular tight frame (ETF) in a space of dimension $d-1$. What is the number of vectors in this ETF, expressed as a function of $d$?

Consider finite graphs where each edge is colored with one of two colors, 1 or 2. A $k$-majority partition of such a 2-edge-colored graph $G=(V,E)$ is a partition of $V$ into $k$ disjoint subsets such that for each color $c \in \{1,2\}$, every vertex $v \in V$ has at least as many incident edges of color $c$ connecting to vertices outside its own subset as it has incident edges of color $c$ connecting to vertices inside its own subset. What is the minimum integer $k$ such that every 2-edge-colored graph is guaranteed to admit a $k$-majority partition?

The geodesic complexity of a length space $X$ is the minimal integer $k$ such that there is a partition of $X \times X$ into $k+1$ locally compact sets, on each of which there exists a continuous choice of a shortest path connecting any given start and end point. What is the geodesic complexity of the surface of a regular octahedron (equipped with the flat metric)?

Consider the following dynamics on a connected graph $(V,E)$ with $n$ vertices. Given $p>1$ and an initial opinion profile $f_0:V \to [0,1]$, at each integer step $t \ge 1$ a uniformly random vertex $v=v_t$ is selected, and its opinion is updated to the value $f_{t}(v)$ that minimizes the sum $\sum_{w \sim v} |f_t(v)-f_{t-1}(w)|^p$ over neighbours $w$ of $v$, while $f_t(u) = f_{t-1}(u)$ for all $u \neq v_t$. It is known that the number of steps needed to reduce the oscillation of $f_t$ below $\varepsilon>0$ is at most $n^{\beta_p}$ (up to logarithmic factors in $n$ and $\varepsilon$). What is the optimal exponent $\beta_p$ as a function of $p$?

The domatic number of a graph $G$ is the maximum number of pairwise disjoint dominating sets of $G$. The fractional domatic number $D_f(G)$ is defined as the LP-relaxation of the domatic number. Let $\mathcal{G}$ be the class of all connected graphs with minimum degree at least $2$. What is the exact value of $\sup_{S \subset \mathcal{G}, |S| < \infty} \inf_{G \in \mathcal{G} \setminus S} D_f(G)$?

For a family $\mathcal{F} \subseteq 2^{[n]}$ and distinct elements $i, j\in [n]$, define $\mathcal{F}(i, ar{j})=\{F\setminus\{i\}: F\in \mathcal{F}, F\cap\{i,j\}=\{i\}\}$. The sturdiness $ eta(\mathcal{F})$ is defined as $\min_{i
eq j} |\mathcal{F}(i, ar{j})|$. A family $\mathcal{F} \subseteq 2^{[n]}$ is called an IU-family if $F\cap F'
eq \emptyset$ and $F\cup F' 
eq [n]$ for all $F,F'\in \mathcal{F}$. What is the maximum possible value of the sturdiness $ eta(\mathcal{F})$ for an IU-family $\mathcal{F} \subseteq 2^{[n]}$, given as a function of $n$?

Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$. The mean subtree order of $G$ is the average number of vertices over all subtrees of $G$. Among all connected graphs of order $n$, which graph is proven to minimize the mean subtree order? Provide your answer as the standard pure LaTeX symbol for the graph family, using a capitalized letter with the subscript $n$ (for example, $K_n$, $C_n$, or $S_n$).

Let $n(k,d)$ denote the maximum number of pairwise $k$-neighborly axis-aligned boxes in $\mathbb{R}^d$, where two such boxes are said to be $k$-neighborly if their intersection has dimension at least $d-k$ and at most $d-1$. For any fixed non-negative integer $s$, as $d \to \infty$, the maximum number of pairwise $(d-s)$-neighborly boxes is asymptotic to $C(s) 2^d$, meaning $n(d-s,d) \sim C(s) 2^d$. What is the explicit expression for $C(s)$?

Let $N(G, K_s)$ denote the number of copies of the complete graph $K_s$ in a simple graph $G$. For each vertex $v \in V(G)$, let $c(v)$ be the length of the longest cycle that contains $v$, or $2$ if $v$ is not part of any cycle. Let $c(u)$ denote the circumference of $G$. A tight upper bound on $N(G, K_s)$ can be established in the form:
$$ N(G, K_s) \leq \left(\sum_{v\in V(G)} g(c(v), s)\right) - g(c(u), s) $$
where the equality is attained for a class of extremal graphs. What is the explicit algebraic expression for the function $g(c, s)$?

Let $X$ be a smooth, complex Fano 4-fold. There exists a minimal integer $N$ such that any smooth, complex Fano 4-fold with Picard number $\rho(X) > N$ is necessarily a product of del Pezzo surfaces. What is the value of $N$?

Consider algorithms with integral action for solving online optimization problems characterized by quadratic cost functions with a time-varying optimal point described by an $(n-1)$-th order polynomial. By the internal model principle, the algorithms incorporate a discrete-time $n$-th order integrator in order to achieve exact tracking. Let $\kappa$ be the condition number for the set of cost functions under consideration. What is the fundamental convergence rate bound (expressed as the optimal asymptotic convergence factor or spectral radius) for the class of linear gradient-based algorithms exactly tracking the time-varying optimal point?

Consider the upwind discontinuous Galerkin (DG) method applied to the time-dependent radiative transfer equation (RTE) with constant cross sections. When using piecewise $k$-th order polynomials for the approximation, what is the exact theoretical order of superconvergence proven for the global $L^2$ and negative-order Sobolev norms?
Let $G = (\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z})^n$. What is the maximum possible size of a subset $H \subseteq G$ such that there are no three distinct elements $x, y, z \in H$ satisfying $x + y + z = 0$?
Up to isomorphism, how many imprimitive finite quaternionic reflection groups of rank two with order $192$ and exactly $22$ reflections exist?

Let $n \ge 3$ be an integer. A cycle-edge cover of a graph $G$ is a collection of cycles and edges in $G$ such that every edge of $G$ belongs to at least one cycle or edge in the collection. Let $c(G)$ denote the minimum size (number of cycles and edges) of a cycle-edge cover of $G$. Over all $n$-vertex graphs $G$ that contain at least one cycle, what is the exact maximum value of $c(G)$ as a function of $n$?

Let $\mathbb{T}_n^d$ be the discrete Euclidean torus of dimension $d$ and side length $n$. Consider a random burning procedure on $\mathbb{T}_n^d$ where, at each step, a new point is set on fire. The choice of the new point, conditionally on the past, is uniformly distributed on the complement of the set of vertices burned in the previous steps. Concurrently, the fire propagates deterministically at unit speed along the edges of the graph. Let the random burning number $B_n$ be the first step at which all vertices of the torus are burned. As $n \to \infty$, $B_n$ is asymptotic in probability to $T n^\alpha$ for some constant $T \in (0, \infty)$ depending only on $d$. What is the exponent $\alpha$ as a function of $d$?

Let $G$ be a minimally $\mathcal{R}_d$-connected graph, meaning its $d$-dimensional generic rigidity matroid is connected and minimal with respect to edge deletion. A graph is $\mathcal{R}_k$-independent if its $k$-dimensional generic rigidity matroid is free. By Maxwell's theorem, any $\mathcal{R}_k$-independent graph satisfies the sparsity condition $|E(H)| \leq k|V(H)| - \binom{k+1}{2}$ for all subgraphs $H$ with $|V(H)| \geq k+1$. It has been shown that every minimally $\mathcal{R}_d$-connected graph $G$ is $\mathcal{R}_{d+1}$-independent. Up to isomorphism, what is the unique graph that can appear as a subgraph of $G$ and satisfy Maxwell's criterion for $\mathcal{R}_{d+1}$-independence with equality? Give the answer using standard complete graph notation in terms of $d$.

Let $R(G_1, \dots, G_k)$ denote the multicolor Ramsey number, defined as the smallest integer $n$ such that every $k$-coloring of the edges of the complete graph $K_n$ contains a monochromatic copy of $G_i$ in color $i$, where $C_m$ denotes a cycle on $m$ vertices. What is the exact value of the Ramsey number $R(C_3, C_6, C_6)$?
What is the Lorentzian Hausdorff dimension of the sub-Lorentzian Heisenberg group?

Consider the double phase functional $u \mapsto \int_\Omega \left(|\nabla u|^p + a(x)|\nabla u|^q\right)\,dx$, where $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^N$ is a bounded Lipschitz domain, $1 \leq p \leq q < \infty$, and $a(x) \geq 0$. If the weight function $a$ belongs to the space $C^{k, \alpha}(U)$ for some open set $U \supset \Omega$ (with $k$ a non-negative integer and $\alpha \in (0, 1]$), there is an optimal upper bound $q_{\max}$ on the exponent $q$ such that the Lavrentiev gap is absent for all $q \leq q_{\max}$. What is the formula for $q_{\max}$ in terms of $p, N, k,$ and $\alpha$?

Consider branching Brownian motion in the two-dimensional hyperbolic space $\mathbb{H}^2$ with branching rate $\beta > 0$. As hyperbolic Brownian motion is transient, the normalized empirical measure of the branching Brownian motion converges to a random measure $\mu_\infty$ on the boundary $\partial \mathbb{H}^2$. What is the almost sure Hausdorff dimension of the support of $\mu_\infty$, conditionally on the survival of the branching process, as a function of $\beta$?

Let $p$ be a prime. Define the restricted totient sum $\Delta(x,p) = \sum_{\substack{k\le x\\ p \mid k}} \varphi(k)$, where $\varphi$ is Euler's totient function. As $x \to \infty$, the asymptotic expansion of $\Delta(x,p)$ is known to take the form $C(p) x^2 + O(x \log x)$. What is the explicit formula for the coefficient $C(p)$?

Let $G$ be a simple graph without isolated vertices, with order $n(G)$, size $m(G)$, first Zagreb index $M_1(G)$, and second Zagreb index $M_2(G)$. A well-known conjecture states that $\frac{M_1(G)}{n(G)} \leq \frac{M_2(G)}{m(G)}$. While this conjecture is known to hold for chemical graphs (where the maximum degree is at most $4$), it fails for general graphs. Let $\mathcal{G}$ be the class of simple graphs without isolated vertices such that the difference between the maximum degree $\Delta(G)$ and the minimum degree $\delta(G)$ is at most $3$. It has been proven that any graph $G \in \mathcal{G}$ that serves as a counterexample to the conjecture must have uniquely determined values for its minimum and maximum degrees. What is the ordered pair $(\delta(G), \Delta(G))$ for any such counterexample?

For a digraph $H$ and an integer $k \ge 1$, define the chromatic profile of $H$, denoted by $\delta_\chi^{+}(H,k)$, to be the infimum $d$ such that any $H$-free digraph $D$ on $n$ vertices with minimum out-degree $\delta^{+}(D) \ge dn$ must be $k$-colorable. Let $T_r$ denote the transitive tournament on $r$ vertices. What is the exact value of $\delta_\chi^{+}(T_r, r-1)$ as a function of $r$?

Let $T$ be an $n$-vertex tree with maximum degree $\Delta(T)$, and let its bipartition classes have sizes $t_1$ and $t_2$, where $t_1 \ge t_2$. It has been proven that there exists a constant $c>0$ such that for every such tree satisfying $\Delta(T) \le cn$, its two-color Ramsey number $R(T)$ is given by an exact formula depending only on $t_1$ and $t_2$. What is this formula for $R(T)$?

Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^N$ (with $N \geq 2$) be a bounded Lipschitz domain with boundary $\Gamma$. It has been established that if a function $u$ is harmonic in $\Omega$ and its trace vanishes on $\Gamma$, then $u = 0$ uniquely, provided $u \in H^{1/2}(\Omega)$ or $u \in W^{1, p}(\Omega)$. What is the specific exponent $p$ given in this uniqueness criterion, expressed as a function of the spatial dimension $N$?

Consider the isotropic $L_p$ Minkowski problem in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ for $n \geq 3$. It has been established that $S_2$-isotropic solutions $K \in \mathcal{K}_+^2$ are unique (i.e., $K$ is the unit ball) for the parameter range $\frac{1-3n^2}{2n}\leq p<-n$ provided that the second eigenvalue of the negative Hilbert-Brunn-Minkowski operator satisfies $\lambda_2(-L_K) \geq f(n,p)$. Furthermore, assuming the origin-centred condition, this uniqueness result is extended to the range $-2n-1 \leq p<-n$ under the condition $\lambda_2(-L_K) \geq \frac{-p-1}{n-1}$. What is the exact rational function $f(n,p)$?
Let $S_p$ be the $p$-convexified Schreier space for $1 \leq p < \infty$. Every subsymmetric basic sequence in $S_p$ is equivalent to the unit vector basis of which standard classical sequence space?

Clobber is an alternate-turn two-player game played on a path graph with each node occupied by a black stone ($x$), a white stone ($o$), or empty ($-$). Player Left has black stones ($x$) and player Right has white stones ($o$). On a turn, a player takes one of their stones that is adjacent to an opponent's stone and clobbers it (replaces it with their own stone). Whoever cannot move loses. An even-length alternating-color linear clobber position starting with $o$ is a starting board state consisting entirely of an alternating sequence of $x$'s and $o$'s of even length, where the first stone is $o$. It is a theorem that for every even-length alternating-color linear clobber position starting with $o$ except exactly one, the first player has a winning strategy. What is this unique exceptional position? Express your answer as a string of $x$'s and $o$'s.

Consider the set of all right-angled Coxeter groups acting discretely and isometrically on hyperbolic 3-space $\mathbb{H}^3$. Let $V_{\min}$ be the minimum covolume among all such groups. What is the exact value of $V_{\min}$? (Express your answer using the standard single capital letter symbol for the named mathematical constant it equals).

Let $X$ be a closed connected non-orientable PL $4$-manifold. It is a theorem that $X$ is a simple branched covering of the twisted $S^3$-bundle $S^1 \tilde{\times} S^3$ if and only if a specific characteristic class of $X$ admits an integral lift. What is this characteristic class?

Consider an $n \times n$ Sudoku puzzle grid partitioned into blocks of size $m \times l$ (where $n = m \cdot l$). To model a partially filled puzzle with $k$ unknown cells, a system of linear equations is constructed where each equation corresponds to the known sum of the unknown elements in a particular row, column, or block. In terms of $n, m,$ and $l$, what is the maximum possible value of $k$ for which the coefficient matrix of this linear system can have full column rank?

Let $S$ be the set of all positive integers $d$ such that the Lebesgue measure is the unique minimizer (up to constant multiples) for the doubling constant among all doubling measures on the Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^d$. What is the set $S$?

Let $P$ be a partially ordered set with $n$ elements. For distinct $x, y \in P$, let $p(x \prec y)$ be the probability that $x$ precedes $y$ in a uniformly chosen linear extension of $P$.
